Professor View 01 / 27
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Walyson Ginaldo Silva

Business English

B1 · Intermediário Santos, SP · Sucafina 48 aulas · 2x semana 90 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Walyson Ginaldo Silva

Idade

29 anos

Cidade

Santos, SP

Profissão

Profissional na Sucafina (commodities agrícolas / café)

Nível

B1 (Intermediário)

Foco

Business English + fluência oral em multinacional

Frequência

2x por semana (segundas e quartas, 07h)

Total de Aulas

48 aulas de 90 minutos

Personagem-Jornada

De: Profissional B1 que se comunica mas sente que "a maioria das pessoas no meu nível fica tranquila" e não quer ser essa maioria.

Para: Profissional que usa expressões idiomáticas com naturalidade, refina a pronúncia e se destaca em reuniões e conversas com hierarquia em ambiente multinacional. Em 48 aulas.

Promessa Transformadora

De quem "trava na hora certa" para quem "se destaca na multinacional". Em 48 aulas, Walyson vai do inglês que funciona para o inglês que impressiona. Como disse o próprio Walyson: "Caí para dentro do curso."

Forças
  • Motivação intrínseca reforçada por modelo de sucesso interno (diretor)
  • Base B1 sólida, comunicação funcional já estabelecida
  • Alta abertura ao aprendizado, sem resistências declaradas
  • Contexto profissional de alta exposição em multinacional
  • Viagens anuais internacionais como laboratório de prática
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Interferência prosódica do português (acento oxítono)
  • Lacuna em expressões idiomáticas e linguagem figurativa
  • Ausência de deadline ou evento-alvo (risco de acomodação)
  • Tempo de estudo fora das aulas não informado

Currículo Completo — 48 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Walyson? Diagnostic + Self-IntroductionPresent Simple routines, Present Perfect trajectory, word stressDiagnostic elicitation + 2-min self-intro3-min audio intro + 5 sentences about workweek
2My Workday at SucafinaPresent Simple routines, adverbs of frequencyDescribe daily schedule + role-play Monday morningWritten daily routine + record audio
3The Coffee Supply ChainPassive voice intro, commodity vocabularyExplain coffee journey from origin to cupResearch and present one coffee origin
4Meeting the TeamIntroductions, present continuous for projectsRole-play introducing colleagues at meetingRecord team introduction
5Emails That Get ResultsFormal register, hedging languageWrite and analyze professional emailsDraft 2 emails: request + follow-up
6Numbers, Prices, and ContractsLarge numbers, percentages, comparativesNegotiate prices in a trading scenarioPractice reading numbers aloud
7Reporting to the DirectorPresent Perfect for updates, linking wordsGive a status update to your managerRecord a 2-min status update
8Handling Phone CallsPhone expressions, indirect questionsSimulate incoming and outgoing callsPractice phone expressions with audio
9REVIEW: Blocks 1-8Consolidation of all structuresDiagnostic comparison + celebrationSelf-assessment journal
10Meetings That MatterModal verbs (should, could, might)Run a team meeting in EnglishWrite meeting agenda + minutes
11Agreeing and DisagreeingDiplomatic disagreement, softenersDebate a business decisionRecord diplomatic responses
12Making PresentationsSignposting language, sequencingDeliver a 5-min presentationPrepare slides + script
13Data and ChartsDescribing trends, comparatives/superlativesAnalyze and present market dataDescribe 2 charts from the news
14Problem-Solving at WorkFirst conditional, suggesting solutionsRole-play solving a logistics crisisWrite problem-solution email
15Negotiating DealsSecond conditional, negotiation phrasesNegotiate a coffee contractListen to negotiation podcast + notes
16Cross-Cultural CommunicationIndirect language, cultural awarenessRole-play with international partnersResearch cultural tips for 2 countries
17REVIEW: Blocks 10-16ConsolidationMock meeting + feedbackSelf-assessment journal
18Travel: Airports and FlightsTravel vocabulary, polite requestsNavigate an international airportRecord airport dialogues
19Travel: Hotels and Check-inHotel vocabulary, complaintsCheck in, report problems, check outWrite hotel review
20Travel: Restaurants and FoodFood vocabulary, ordering, preferencesOrder a business dinnerRecord restaurant role-play
21Small Talk MasteryConversation starters, follow-up questionsPractice small talk in multiple contextsWatch TED Talk on conversation skills
22Idioms in Business10 business idioms in contextUse idioms in realistic scenariosFind idioms in a podcast episode
23Giving and Receiving FeedbackReported speech, constructive languageRole-play feedback conversationsWrite feedback using sandwich method
24REVIEW: Blocks 18-23ConsolidationTravel + business simulationSelf-assessment journal
25Industry Vocabulary: Coffee TradingSpecialized terminology, collocationsExplain the trading processCreate a glossary of 15 terms
26Sustainability in CommoditiesOpinion language, cause and effectDiscuss sustainability initiativesRead article + prepare summary
27Video Calls and Virtual MeetingsVirtual meeting etiquette, tech vocabularyRun a virtual meetingRecord a virtual presentation
28Networking EventsNetworking phrases, follow-upSimulate a networking eventWrite follow-up emails
29Phrasal Verbs in Business15 key phrasal verbsReplace formal verbs with phrasal verbsCreate sentences with 10 phrasal verbs
30REVIEW: Blocks 25-29ConsolidationIndustry presentation + Q&ASelf-assessment journal
31Explaining ProcessesPassive voice, sequencing adverbsExplain a work process step by stepWrite a process description
32Handling Difficult ConversationsDiplomatic language, de-escalationRole-play conflict resolutionListen to conflict resolution podcast
33Job Interviews and Career GrowthSTAR method, professional narrativeMock interview for promotionPrepare 3 STAR answers
34Financial English BasicsFinancial vocabulary, reading reportsAnalyze a financial summaryRead a financial report section
35Storytelling in BusinessNarrative tenses, dramatic presentTell a compelling business storyPrepare a 3-min business story
36REVIEW: Blocks 31-35ConsolidationFull interview + storytellingSelf-assessment journal
37Advanced Email WritingFormal vs informal register, nuanceWrite emails for different audiencesRewrite 3 emails in different registers
38Persuasion and InfluencePersuasive language, rhetorical devicesPitch an idea to managementWatch TED Talk on persuasion + notes
39Conference and Summit EnglishPresentation skills, Q&A handlingDeliver a summit-style presentationPrepare a 5-min presentation
40Global Markets and TrendsDescribing trends, future predictionsDiscuss global commodity market trendsRead market report + present
41Pronunciation IntensiveWord stress, connected speech, intonationShadowing + pronunciation drillsRecord 10 sentences with focus on stress
42REVIEW: Blocks 37-41ConsolidationFull presentation + Q&A simulationSelf-assessment journal
43Leadership CommunicationMotivational language, delegationLead a team briefingRecord a team motivation speech
44Crisis Management EnglishUrgency language, clear instructionsHandle a supply chain crisisWrite crisis communication
45Social English: Beyond WorkCasual register, humor, travel storiesChat about travel, hobbies, weekendRecord a travel story
46Mock Summit: Full SimulationAll structures integratedFull-day simulation: meeting + presentation + networkingSelf-reflection journal
47Final Diagnostic + Gap AnalysisAll structuresFinal diagnostic + comparison with Aula 1Write personal growth narrative
48Celebration + Continuity PlanConsolidationCelebration + next steps planPersonal English plan for the future
Regras de Cancelamento
  • Cancelamentos com mais de 24h de antecedência: aula reagendada sem custo.
  • Cancelamentos com menos de 24h: aula cobrada normalmente.
  • No-show (ausência sem aviso): aula cobrada normalmente.
  • Atrasos: a aula encerra no horário previsto, sem extensão.
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Walyson? -- First Impressions at the Coffee Summit

Professional self-introduction vocabulary and diagnostic baseline. Key words: commodity, sourcing, stakeholder, report to, oversee, headquarters, negotiate, expertise. Structures: present simple for routines, present perfect for trajectory.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Commodity -- a raw material or product bought and sold in large quantities
"Coffee is one of the most traded commodities in the world." (Commodity / matéria-prima)
Sourcing -- the process of finding and obtaining materials or products
"Our sourcing team works directly with farmers in Brazil." (Aquisição / sourcing)
Stakeholder -- a person with an interest or concern in a business or project
"We briefed all stakeholders before the quarterly review." (Parte interessada)
Report to -- to work under someone's supervision, to have a manager above you
"I report directly to the director of operations." (Responder a / reportar para)
Oversee -- to watch over and manage work, a project, or people
"She oversees the entire export operation from Santos." (Supervisionar)
Headquarters -- the main offices of an organization or company
"Sucafina's headquarters are in Geneva, Switzerland." (Sede / matriz)
Negotiate -- to discuss something in order to reach an agreement
"We need to negotiate better terms with the shipping company." (Negociar)
Expertise -- special skill or knowledge in a particular area
"His expertise in green coffee grading is well known in the industry." (Especialização / expertise)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition in English.

Commodity
Sourcing
Stakeholder
Report to
Oversee
Headquarters
Negotiate
Expertise

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson works at Sucafina, a Swiss multinational that trades coffee commodities around the world. Every day, he coordinates with stakeholders from different countries and reports directly to the director. He oversees key operations at the Santos office and helps negotiate contracts with international sourcing partners. "I have worked with coffee trading for several years," he explains. "The industry has grown a lot, and my expertise in the Brazilian market has helped us connect with new origins. Our headquarters are in Geneva, but Santos is where the action happens."

1. Which tense does Walyson use to describe his daily routine at Sucafina?
A Present simple: "works", "coordinates", "reports"
B Present perfect: "has worked", "has grown"
C Past simple: "worked", "coordinated"
2. Why does Walyson say "I have worked with coffee trading for several years" using the present perfect?
A Because it happened at a specific time last year.
B Because the experience started in the past and continues to the present.
C Because it is happening right now.
3. In the sentence "Santos is where the action happens," which tense is used and why?
A Present perfect, because it connects past to present.
B Present simple, because it describes a permanent fact.
C Past simple, because Santos was important before.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Perfect

GRAMMAR

Quando usar cada tempo verbal. / When to use each tense.

Tense / TempoUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
Present SimpleRotinas, fatos permanentes / Routines, permanent factsWalyson works at Sucafina.
Present Simple (3rd person)He/she/it + verbo com -s / adds -sHe reports to the director.
Present PerfectExperiência acumulada, conexão passado-presente / Accumulated experienceI have worked with coffee for years.
Present Perfect (structure)have/has + past participleThe industry has grown a lot.
Diferença-chave / Key differencePresent simple = o que você FAZ sempre ("I work"). Present perfect = o que você já FEZ até agora ("I have worked for years").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Coffee is one of the most traded in the world."
"I directly to the director of operations."
"She the entire export operation from Santos."
"Our are in Geneva, Switzerland."
"We need to better terms with the shipping company."
"His in coffee grading is well known."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Sarah asks about the Santos port operations.
?They exchange business cards and agree to follow up.
?Walyson introduces himself at the summit.
?They discuss the new crop season.
?He explains his role at Sucafina.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I work at Sucafina, a multinational in the coffee industry.
Trabalho na Sucafina, uma multinacional do setor de café.
I report directly to the director.
Eu respondo diretamente ao diretor.
I have worked with coffee commodities for several years.
Trabalho com commodities de café há vários anos.
Our headquarters are in Geneva, but I am based in Santos.
Nossa sede fica em Genebra, mas eu sou baseado em Santos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A new international partner at a coffee summit asks you to describe your role. What do you say?
A "I do coffee things at Sucafina."
B "I work at Sucafina. I oversee operations at our Santos office and report directly to the director."
C "I buy coffee for a company."
Someone at a networking event asks: "How long have you been in the coffee industry?" You respond:
A "I am in coffee since 2019."
B "I have been in the coffee industry for several years. I have gained a lot of expertise in sourcing and trading."
C "I worked coffee many years."
Your director asks where Sucafina's headquarters are. The most professional response is:
A "Our main place is in Switzerland somewhere."
B "We have headquarters."
C "Our headquarters are in Geneva, Switzerland. We also have offices in several countries, including Brazil."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at an international coffee summit. A colleague from the Switzerland headquarters walks up to you. Introduce yourself: your name, your city, your company, your role, and one thing you are proud of in your career. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1I work at Sucafina, a coffee trading company.Trabalho na Sucafina, uma empresa de trading de café.
2I report directly to the director.Eu respondo diretamente ao diretor.
3I am based in Santos, Brazil.Eu sou baseado em Santos, Brasil.
4I have worked with coffee commodities for several years.Trabalho com commodities de café há vários anos.
5It is a pleasure to meet you.É um prazer te conhecer.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

My Workday at Sucafina -- Daily Routines and Adverbs of Frequency

Present Simple for routines + adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never) and their position in the sentence. New vocabulary: schedule, commute, shift, deadline, spreadsheet, shipment, warehouse, inventory, forecast, batch, catch up on, wrap up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Schedule -- a plan that lists the times of your tasks and meetings
"I check my schedule every morning before the market opens." (Agenda / cronograma)
Commute -- the regular trip between your home and your workplace
"My commute to the office takes about forty minutes." (Trajeto casa-trabalho)
Shift -- a fixed period of working hours
"I always start my shift with a strong coffee." (Turno)
Deadline -- the latest time by which a task must be finished
"There is always a tight deadline at the end of the month." (Prazo)
Spreadsheet -- a digital sheet of rows and columns for numbers
"I open the spreadsheet to check the weekend numbers." (Planilha)
Shipment -- a load of goods sent together to a destination
"Three shipments leave the warehouse on Wednesday." (Carregamento / remessa)
Warehouse -- a large building where goods are stored
"We keep the green coffee in the warehouse near the port." (Armazém / galpão)
Inventory -- the full amount of stock a company holds
"I review the inventory before the market opens." (Estoque / inventário)
Forecast -- a prediction of future numbers or events
"I check the price forecast around noon." (Previsão)
Batch -- a quantity prepared or handled at one time
"I prepare the next batch for export." (Lote)
Catch up on -- to do work that you are behind on
"I usually catch up on my emails after lunch." (Colocar em dia)
Wrap up -- to finish or complete something
"I always wrap up the day with a short summary." (Finalizar / encerrar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Schedule
Commute
Shift
Deadline
Spreadsheet
Shipment
Warehouse
Inventory
Forecast
Batch

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson always starts his day early. His commute to the Sucafina office usually takes forty minutes, so he often arrives before seven. He always opens his spreadsheet first and checks the weekend numbers. He usually reviews the inventory in the warehouse, because the team always needs to know how much coffee they hold. He sometimes joins a call with Geneva, but he rarely has a quiet morning. There is always a deadline, so he never leaves without a clear plan. At the end of the day, he always wraps up with a short summary.

1. Where does the adverb "always" go in these sentences?
A Before the main verb (he always opens).
B At the very end of the sentence.
C Right after the object.
2. How often does Walyson have a quiet morning?
A Always.
B Rarely.
C Usually.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "He reviews usually the inventory."
B "He usually reviews the inventory."
C "Usually he reviews inventory the."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Adverbs of Frequency

GRAMMAR

Usados para dizer com que frequência algo acontece. / Used to say how often something happens.

Rule / RegraDetail / DetalheExample / Exemplo
Before the main verbAntes do verbo principalI always check the schedule.
After the verb "to be"Depois do verbo to beI am usually early.
Frequency scalealways 100% · usually 90% · often 70% · sometimes 50% · rarely 10% · never 0%I sometimes join the call.
NegativesCom "do not / does not" (não use never com not)He does not usually work late.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o advérbio de frequência vem antes do verbo principal, mas depois do verbo to be. Nunca "I go always" -- diga "I always go".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"My to the office takes about forty minutes."
"I always start my with a strong coffee."
"I open the to check the weekend numbers."
"I review the in the warehouse every morning."
"There is always a tight at the end of the month."
"At the end of the day, I with a short summary."

Stage 2: Put the Day in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Walyson's workday in the correct order.

?In the morning, he reviews the warehouse inventory and catches up on his emails.
?Walyson wakes up early and commutes to the Sucafina office in Santos.
?At the end of the day, he wraps up by writing a short summary for his director.
?He starts his shift with a coffee and opens his spreadsheet to check the numbers.
?Around noon, he checks the price forecast and prepares the next batch for export.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on the word stress.

My commute to the office usually takes forty minutes.
Meu trajeto até o escritório geralmente leva quarenta minutos.
I always start my shift with a strong coffee.
Eu sempre começo meu turno com um café forte.
There is always a tight deadline at the end of the month.
Sempre há um prazo apertado no fim do mês.
I usually catch up on my emails after lunch.
Eu geralmente coloco meus e-mails em dia depois do almoço.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each work situation.

A colleague asks how often you check the inventory. The most natural answer is:
A "I usually check it before the market opens."
B "I am checking it usually before the market."
C "I check usually it before the market opens."
You want to say your commute is forty minutes most days. The best sentence is:
A "My commute usually takes forty minutes."
B "My commute takes usually forty minutes."
C "Usually my commute is take forty minutes."
Where does "always" go in: "I ___ wrap up the day with a summary"?
A "I always wrap up the day with a summary."
B "I wrap up always the day with a summary."
C "I wrap up the day with a summary always."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a typical workday at Sucafina, from your commute in the morning to how you wrap up at the end of the day. Use the present simple and at least four adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never).

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1My commute usually takes forty minutes.Meu trajeto geralmente leva quarenta minutos.
2I always start my shift with a coffee.Eu sempre começo meu turno com um café.
3I often catch up on my emails after lunch.Eu costumo colocar meus e-mails em dia depois do almoço.
4There is always a deadline to meet.Sempre há um prazo a cumprir.
5I always wrap up the day with a short summary.Eu sempre encerro o dia com um breve resumo.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

The Coffee Supply Chain -- Passive Voice (Origin to Cup)

The passive voice (to be + past participle) to describe a process: the coffee is grown, the beans are roasted. New vocabulary: harvest, grower, ripe, mill, grade, roast, blend, origin, export, supply chain, brew, trace.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Harvest -- to pick the ripe coffee cherries from the plants
"The cherries are harvested by hand between May and September." (Colher / colheita)
Grower -- a farmer or company that grows the coffee
"Each lot is traced back to a single grower." (Produtor / cafeicultor)
Ripe -- fully developed and ready to be picked
"The fruit is picked only when it is ripe." (Maduro)
Mill -- to process the cherries and remove the outer fruit
"At the mill, the beans are dried and cleaned." (Beneficiar)
Grade -- to sort the beans by size and quality
"The beans are graded before they are exported." (Classificar)
Roast -- to heat the green beans until they turn brown
"The beans are roasted in small batches." (Torrar)
Blend -- a mixture of beans from different origins
"Each blend is documented before it is roasted." (Blend / mistura)
Origin -- the place or country where the coffee is grown
"This origin is known for a sweet, balanced cup." (Origem)
Export -- to send goods to another country to be sold
"The best lots are exported from the port of Santos." (Exportar)
Supply chain -- the whole path a product follows from producer to consumer
"He walks the buyer through the supply chain." (Cadeia de suprimentos)
Brew -- to make coffee by passing hot water through the ground beans
"A small sample is brewed for the buyers." (Coar / preparar o café)
Trace -- to follow a product back to its source
"Every step is traced from the farm to the port." (Rastrear)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Harvest
Grower
Ripe
Mill
Grade
Roast
Origin
Export
Brew
Trace

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every morning, fresh coffee is enjoyed around the world, but the journey is long. The coffee is grown on small farms, and the ripe cherries are harvested by hand. At the mill, the fruit is removed and the green beans are dried in the sun. Then the beans are graded by quality, and the best lots are exported from Santos. Abroad, the coffee is roasted, a blend is created, and finally it is brewed into your cup. Every step is recorded, so each bag can be traced back to the grower.

1. In "the coffee is grown", which two parts form the passive?
A A form of "to be" + the past participle (is grown).
B Two nouns next to each other.
C An adverb plus a verb.
2. Why does the writer use the passive so often in this text?
A To name the workers who do each task.
B To focus on the process and the action, not on who does it.
C Because the actions happened only once in the past.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "The cherries are harvest by hand."
B "The cherries are harvested by hand."
C "The cherries harvest by hand."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Passive Voice

GRAMMAR

Usada para focar na ação, não em quem a faz. / Used to focus on the action, not on the doer.

Rule / RegraDetail / DetalheExample / Exemplo
am / is / are + past participleForma do passivo no presenteCoffee is grown at high altitude.
Agent with "by"O agente é opcional, introduzido por "by"The cherries are harvested by hand.
Focus on the actionUse quando importa a ação, não quem fazThe beans are roasted and ground.
Irregular participlesgrow→grown, take→taken, send→sentThe cherries are taken to the mill.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o passivo usa to be + past participle. Nunca "the beans are roast" -- diga "the beans are roasted".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The coffee is on small mountain farms."
"The ripe cherries are by hand."
"At the mill, the green beans are by quality."
"The best lots are from the port of Santos."
"Abroad, the beans are in small batches."
"Every lot is back to the grower."

Stage 2: Put the Journey in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the coffee supply chain in the correct order.

?The best lots are exported from the port of Santos to roasters abroad.
?The coffee is grown on mountain farms and the ripe cherries are harvested by hand.
?Finally, the coffee is brewed into the cup and traced back to the grower.
?The cherries are taken to the mill, where the beans are dried and graded by quality.
?At their destination, the green beans are roasted and ground.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on the word stress.

The coffee is grown on small mountain farms.
O café é cultivado em pequenas fazendas nas montanhas.
The ripe cherries are harvested by hand.
As cerejas maduras são colhidas à mão.
The beans are graded by quality before they are exported.
Os grãos são classificados por qualidade antes de serem exportados.
Every lot is traced back to a single grower.
Cada lote é rastreado até um único produtor.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each work situation.

A buyer asks where your coffee comes from. The most natural answer is:
A "The coffee is grown on small farms in the mountains."
B "The coffee is grow in the mountains by us."
C "We are growing the coffee mountains."
You want to explain how the quality is checked. The best sentence is:
A "The beans are graded by size and quality at the mill."
B "The beans grade by size and quality at the mill."
C "The beans are grade by size at the mill quality."
The buyer asks if a bag can be checked back to one farm. You answer:
A "Yes, every lot is traced back to a single grower."
B "Yes, every lot are traced back the grower."
C "Yes, we tracing every lot to grower."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Explain the coffee supply chain to a new buyer, from origin to cup. Describe how the coffee is grown, harvested, milled, graded, exported, roasted, and brewed. Use the passive voice (to be + past participle) at each step, and end by saying how a lot is traced back to the grower.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1The coffee is grown on small mountain farms.O café é cultivado em pequenas fazendas nas montanhas.
2The ripe cherries are harvested by hand.As cerejas maduras são colhidas à mão.
3The beans are milled, dried, and graded by quality.Os grãos são beneficiados, secos e classificados por qualidade.
4The best lots are exported from the port of Santos.Os melhores lotes são exportados pelo porto de Santos.
5Every lot is traced back to a single grower.Cada lote é rastreado até um único produtor.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

Meeting the Team -- Introductions and the Present Continuous

Introducing colleagues and describing current projects with the present continuous (to be + verb-ing): she is leading, we are launching. New vocabulary: colleague, supervisor, counterpart, department, role, teammate, in charge of, handle, lead, launch, set up, reach out.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Colleague -- a person you work with in the same company
"My colleague is handling the new accounts." (Colega)
Supervisor -- the person who manages your work and your team
"My supervisor is leading the shipping plan." (Supervisor / chefe)
Counterpart -- a person with the same role in another company or office
"My counterpart in Geneva is reviewing the contracts." (Equivalente / homólogo)
Department -- a section of a company, such as sales or finance
"He is in charge of the logistics department." (Departamento / setor)
Role -- the function or position a person has in a team
"My role is quality and traceability." (Função / papel)
Teammate -- a member of your own team
"My teammate is handling the new samples." (Colega de equipe)
In charge of -- responsible for, or having control of, something
"She is in charge of the finance team." (Responsável por)
Handle -- to deal with or manage a task or a problem
"Rafael is handling the quarterly report." (Lidar com / cuidar de)
Lead -- to guide or be the head of a team or project
"I am leading the logistics project this quarter." (Liderar)
Launch -- to start a new project or product
"We are launching a new traceability platform." (Lançar)
Set up -- to arrange or organize something, such as a meeting
"He is setting up the meetings with the buyers." (Organizar / marcar)
Reach out -- to contact someone to ask for help or information
"Our buyer is reaching out to roasters in Germany." (Entrar em contato)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Colleague
Supervisor
Counterpart
Department
Role
Teammate
In charge of
Handle
Lead
Launch
Set up
Reach out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is a busy week at the Santos office. Bruno, the supervisor, is leading the shipping plan for Europe. In quality control, Camila is handling the samples from three new origins. In the next room, Rafael is preparing the quarterly report and is setting up the meetings with the buyers. The whole team is launching a new traceability platform, and the junior buyer is reaching out to roasters in Germany. Meanwhile, in Geneva, the counterpart and her analysts are reviewing the new contracts. Everyone is working hard, because the new project starts next month.

1. In "she is handling", which two parts form the present continuous?
A A form of "to be" + the verb-ing (is handling).
B Two nouns next to each other.
C A past participle after "to be".
2. Why does the writer use the present continuous so often here?
A Because these are routines that happen every day.
B To describe what people are doing right now, this week.
C Because the actions are already finished.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We is launching a new platform."
B "We are launching a new platform."
C "We are launch a new platform."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Present Continuous

GRAMMAR

Usado para ações que acontecem agora ou projetos do momento. / Used for actions happening now or current projects.

Rule / RegraDetail / DetalheExample / Exemplo
am / is / are + verb-ingForma do present continuousShe is leading the project.
Happening nowAção em progresso neste momentoWe are launching a platform.
Current projectsUse com now, this week, at the momentThis week he is setting up the calls.
Spelling of -ingmake→making, run→runningI am preparing the report.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o present continuous usa to be + verb-ing. Nunca "she handling" -- diga "she is handling".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"This is Bruno, my ."
"He is in of the logistics department."
"Camila is the new samples."
"We are a new traceability platform."
"Our buyer is out to roasters in Germany."
"I am up a call with my counterpart."

Stage 2: Put the Meeting in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the team meeting in the correct order.

?Then each colleague explains what they are working on this week.
?First, the manager opens the meeting and introduces the visitor to the team.
?Finally, the manager thanks everyone and closes the meeting.
?Next, each colleague says their role and which department they work in.
?After that, they set up the next call and assign the new tasks.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on the word stress.

This is Bruno, my supervisor.
Este é o Bruno, meu supervisor.
He is in charge of the logistics department.
Ele é responsável pelo departamento de logística.
She is my teammate, and she is handling the samples.
Ela é minha colega de equipe e está cuidando das amostras.
We are launching a new traceability platform.
Estamos lançando uma nova plataforma de rastreabilidade.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each work situation.

A visitor asks who manages your team. The most natural answer is:
A "Bruno is my supervisor. He is in charge of logistics."
B "Bruno is in charge logistics, he my supervisor."
C "Bruno managing logistics is my team."
You want to say what your teammate is doing now. The best sentence is:
A "Camila is handling the samples from three new origins."
B "Camila handling the samples from new origins."
C "Camila is handle the samples three origins."
Your counterpart asks you to arrange a call. You answer:
A "Sure, I am setting up a video call for Tuesday."
B "Sure, I setting up a call Tuesday video."
C "Sure, I am set up a call for video Tuesday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Introduce your real team to a new counterpart. Name two or three colleagues, give each person's role and department, and say what each one is working on right now. Use the present continuous (to be + verb-ing), and end by setting up a call.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1This is Bruno, my supervisor.Este é o Bruno, meu supervisor.
2He is in charge of the logistics department.Ele é responsável pelo departamento de logística.
3She is my teammate, and she is handling the samples.Ela é minha colega de equipe e está cuidando das amostras.
4We are launching a new traceability platform.Estamos lançando uma nova plataforma de rastreabilidade.
5I am setting up a call with my counterpart.Estou marcando uma chamada com meu equivalente.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Emails That Get Results -- Formal Register and Hedging

Writing professional emails with a polite, formal tone and hedging language (could you please, I was wondering if, would it be possible to). New vocabulary: recipient, subject line, attachment, draft, forward, reply, regarding, acknowledge, confirm, clarify, follow up, request.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recipient -- the person who receives an email or message
"The recipient of the email is my counterpart in Geneva." (Destinatário)
Subject line -- the short title at the top of an email
"A clear subject line helps the recipient understand the email." (Assunto)
Attachment -- a file you send together with an email
"Please find the report in the attachment." (Anexo)
Draft -- a first version of an email before you send it
"I am writing a draft of the email now." (Rascunho)
Forward -- to send a received email on to another person
"Could you forward this email to the finance team?" (Encaminhar)
Reply -- to answer an email or a message
"I will reply to your message this afternoon." (Responder)
Regarding -- about, or concerning, a topic
"I am writing regarding the shipment to Europe." (A respeito de / sobre)
Acknowledge -- to confirm that you received something
"Please acknowledge receipt of this email." (Confirmar o recebimento)
Confirm -- to say that something is certain or correct
"Could you confirm the delivery date?" (Confirmar)
Clarify -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify the terms of the contract?" (Esclarecer)
Follow up -- to check on something after a first message
"I am writing to follow up on my last email." (Dar seguimento / retomar)
Request -- to ask for something politely and formally
"I would like to request a copy of the invoice." (Solicitar / pedido)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Recipient
Subject line
Attachment
Draft
Forward
Reply
Regarding
Acknowledge
Confirm
Clarify
Follow up
Request

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson is writing a professional email to a supplier. He keeps a polite, formal tone. He opens softly: I am writing regarding our order for next month. Then he makes his request in a gentle way: I was wondering if you could send the updated price list, and could you please confirm the delivery date? He mentions the file: please find the contract in the attachment. Near the end, he hedges again: would it be possible to reply by Friday? He closes with care: I would appreciate it if you could acknowledge this message. Best regards. A direct order, like "send it now", would sound rude, so Walyson softens every request.

1. Why does Walyson use phrases like "could you please" and "I was wondering if"?
A To make his request softer and more polite (hedging).
B To make the email shorter.
C To give a direct order.
2. How does he open the email politely?
A "Send me the price list now."
B "I am writing regarding our order for next month."
C "Reply fast."
3. Which closing sounds the most formal and polite?
A "Answer me today."
B "Do it soon."
C "I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Hedging and Formal Register

GRAMMAR

Em emails formais, suavizamos os pedidos para soar educado. / In formal emails, we soften requests to sound polite.

When / QuandoPolite form / Forma educadaExample / Exemplo
Small requestPedido pequenoCould you please send the file?
Bigger requestPedido maiorI was wondering if you could help.
Asking permissionPedindo permissãoWould it be possible to meet Friday?
Closing politelyFechamento educadoI would appreciate it if you could reply.

Key point / Ponto-chave: evite ordens diretas ("send it now"). Suavize com could you, I was wondering if, would it be possible to e feche com Best regards.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am writing our order for next month."
"I was if you could send the price list."
"Please find the contract in the ."
"Could you please the delivery date?"
"I am writing to up on my last email."
"I would appreciate it if you could by Friday."

Stage 2: Put the Email in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a professional email in the correct order.

?Then make your request softly, using "could you" or "I was wondering if".
?First, write a short and clear subject line.
?Finally, close with a polite line and your name.
?Next, open the email politely and say what it is regarding.
?After that, mention the attachment and ask the recipient to reply.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, polite tone.

I am writing regarding our order for next month.
Estou escrevendo a respeito do nosso pedido para o próximo mês.
I was wondering if you could send me the price list.
Eu queria saber se você poderia me enviar a lista de preços.
Could you please confirm the delivery date?
Você poderia, por favor, confirmar a data de entrega?
I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday.
Eu agradeceria se você pudesse responder até sexta-feira.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each email situation.

You need to ask a supplier for an updated price list. The most polite request is:
A "I was wondering if you could send me the updated price list."
B "Send the updated price list now."
C "Price list. Fast."
You want to start an email about the shipment to Europe. The best opening is:
A "I am writing regarding the shipment to Europe."
B "About shipment Europe I write."
C "Shipment Europe, answer me."
The supplier has not answered. You write a polite follow-up:
A "I am writing to follow up on my last email. Could you please reply?"
B "Why no answer? Reply now."
C "You forgot my email. Bad."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Dictate a complete professional request email out loud. Start with a clear subject line, open politely with "I am writing regarding...", make a soft request using "could you" or "I was wondering if", mention the attachment, and close with "I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday. Best regards."

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1I am writing regarding our order for next month.Estou escrevendo a respeito do nosso pedido para o próximo mês.
2I was wondering if you could send me the price list.Eu queria saber se você poderia me enviar a lista de preços.
3Please find the contract in the attachment.Segue o contrato em anexo.
4Could you please confirm the delivery date?Você poderia, por favor, confirmar a data de entrega?
5I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday.Eu agradeceria se você pudesse responder até sexta-feira.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Numbers, Prices, and Contracts -- Comparatives and Big Numbers

Talking about prices, large numbers, percentages and comparisons in a trading scenario (cheaper than, more expensive than, increased by 10%). New vocabulary: quote, bid, discount, margin, invoice, volume, rate, estimate, figure, currency, increase, decrease.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Quote -- a stated price you offer for a product or service
"I will send you a quote for one container of coffee." (Cotação / orçamento)
Bid -- an offer to buy something at a certain price
"The buyer made a bid of four thousand dollars per ton." (Oferta de compra / lance)
Discount -- a reduction in the normal price
"For a large order, we can offer a small discount." (Desconto)
Margin -- the profit between the cost and the selling price
"Our margin is small when costs are high." (Margem de lucro)
Invoice -- a document that lists goods and the amount to pay
"Please pay the invoice within thirty days." (Fatura / nota fiscal)
Volume -- the total quantity or amount of something
"A bigger volume usually means a lower price." (Volume / quantidade)
Rate -- a fixed price or amount, for example per ton or per unit
"The rate today is higher than last week." (Taxa / preço unitário)
Estimate -- an approximate calculation of a cost or quantity
"My estimate is that prices will rise next month." (Estimativa)
Figure -- a number, especially in business or finance
"Let me check the final figure on the invoice." (Número / cifra)
Currency -- the money used in a country, like the dollar or the real
"We trade coffee in dollars, the usual currency." (Moeda)
Increase -- to go up; a rise in amount, number or price
"Prices increase when demand is strong." (Aumentar / aumento)
Decrease -- to go down; a fall in amount, number or price
"Prices decrease when the harvest is large." (Diminuir / queda)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Quote
Bid
Discount
Margin
Invoice
Volume
Rate
Estimate
Figure
Currency
Increase
Decrease

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson is preparing a price quote for a buyer in Europe. The buyer wants a large volume, so Walyson can offer a small discount. Coffee is more expensive this season than last year, because the harvest was smaller. Walyson checks the figure twice: his rate is higher than the market average, but it is cheaper than his main rival. He keeps a small margin, so his price is more competitive than before. He sends the invoice in dollars, the usual currency, and writes that prices may increase by ten percent next month. A bigger volume is always better than a small one, because the cost per ton is lower.

1. Why can Walyson offer a discount?
A Because the buyer wants a large volume.
B Because his margin is very big.
C Because the currency is the euro.
2. How does Walyson describe his price compared to his rival?
A It is more expensive than his rival.
B It is cheaper and more competitive than his rival.
C It is the same as the market average.
3. What may happen to prices next month?
A They may decrease by ten percent.
B They will stay exactly the same.
C They may increase by ten percent.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives, Big Numbers and Percentages

GRAMMAR

Comparamos preços e números com comparativos. / We compare prices and numbers with comparatives.

Type / TipoRule / RegraExample / Exemplo
Short adjective+ er + thanOur price is cheaper than the market.
Long adjectivemore + adj + thanCoffee is more expensive than last year.
Irregulargood/badThis rate is better than the last one.
Percentagesincrease / decrease by X%Prices increased by 10% this month.

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca diga "more cheaper" nem "more better". Use só UMA forma comparativa: cheaper, better, more expensive. Números grandes: 4,200 = four thousand two hundred.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Our price is than the market."
"Coffee is more this year than last year."
"A bigger volume means a cost per ton."
"Prices may by ten percent next month."
"Please pay the within thirty days."
"For a large order, we can offer a ."

Stage 2: Put the Price Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of preparing a price in the correct order.

?Then, if the volume is big, offer a small discount on the figure.
?First, check the volume the buyer wants and your margin.
?Finally, send the invoice and confirm the final bid.
?Next, calculate the rate and prepare a clear quote in the right currency.
?After that, compare your price with the market to show it is more competitive.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on clear numbers and comparatives.

Our price is cheaper than the market average.
Nosso preço é mais barato que a média do mercado.
Coffee is more expensive this season than last year.
O café está mais caro nesta safra do que no ano passado.
Prices increased by ten percent last month.
Os preços aumentaram dez por cento no mês passado.
A bigger volume means a lower cost per ton.
Um volume maior significa um custo menor por tonelada.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each trading situation.

A buyer asks for a lower price on a very large order. The best reply is:
A "Because the volume is large, I can offer a small discount."
B "The price is the price. No discount."
C "Small order, big discount."
You want to say your price is good compared to the market:
A "My rate is more competitive than the market average."
B "My rate is more cheaper than market."
C "Price good. You buy."
You need to explain that prices went up this month. You say:
A "Prices increased by eight percent this month."
B "Prices increase eight percents."
C "Prices is more high now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a buyer wants a quote for two containers of coffee. Out loud, give your price. Mention the rate and the currency, say your price is cheaper or more competitive than the market, offer a small discount for the large volume, and say prices may increase by a percentage next month.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1I will send you a quote and the invoice today.Vou te enviar uma cotação e a fatura hoje.
2Our price is more competitive than the market.Nosso preço é mais competitivo que o mercado.
3For a large volume, I can offer a small discount.Para um volume grande, posso oferecer um pequeno desconto.
4Prices increased by ten percent this month.Os preços aumentaram dez por cento este mês.
5We trade in dollars, the usual currency.Negociamos em dólares, a moeda de costume.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Reporting to the Director -- Present Perfect and Linking Words

Giving a clear status update to a manager: present perfect for results (have done, has finished, just/already/yet/so far) and linking words (however, therefore, in addition, as a result). New vocabulary: update, progress, milestone, achieve, target, brief, highlight, pending, status, on track, delay, overview.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Update -- a short report on the latest progress
"I have an update on the Europe project." (Atualização)
Progress -- forward movement toward a goal
"We have made good progress this week." (Progresso)
Milestone -- an important stage or achievement in a project
"We have reached an important milestone." (Marco / etapa importante)
Achieve -- to reach a goal successfully
"We have achieved our target for the quarter." (Alcançar / atingir)
Target -- a result you plan to reach
"Our target is two hundred tons this month." (Meta / alvo)
Brief -- to give someone the key information they need
"Let me brief you on the situation." (Informar / dar um resumo)
Highlight -- to point out the most important part
"I want to highlight one risk in the report." (Destacar / ressaltar)
Pending -- waiting to be done or decided
"Two contracts are still pending." (Pendente)
Status -- the current state or situation of something
"What is the status of the shipment?" (Situação / estado atual)
On track -- going according to plan, on schedule
"The project is on track for next month." (No caminho certo / dentro do prazo)
Delay -- a situation where something happens later than planned
"There has been a small delay on one order." (Atraso)
Overview -- a short, general summary of a situation
"Here is a quick overview of the quarter." (Visão geral / panorama)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Update
Progress
Milestone
Achieve
Target
Brief
Highlight
Pending
Status
On track
Delay
Overview

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson is writing a status update for the director. So far, the team has made good progress. They have already signed two new contracts, and they have reached an important milestone, because they have achieved the target volume for the quarter. In addition, the logistics team has improved the shipping plan, so the project is on track. However, Walyson wants to highlight one problem: the samples from one origin have not arrived yet, therefore there has been a small delay. The status of that order is still pending, but he has contacted the supplier, and as a result they have promised the samples for Monday. He has not finished the full report yet, but he will send a clear overview by Friday.

1. How many contracts has the team signed so far?
A They have already signed two new contracts.
B They have not signed any contracts.
C They have signed ten contracts.
2. Why has there been a small delay?
A Because the director is on holiday.
B Because the samples from one origin have not arrived yet.
C Because the team missed the target.
3. What has Walyson done about the pending order?
A He has cancelled the order.
B He has done nothing yet.
C He has contacted the supplier, and they have promised the samples for Monday.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect and Linking Words

GRAMMAR

Usamos o present perfect para resultados até agora. / We use the present perfect for results up to now.

Form / FormaStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Affirmativehave / has + past participleWe have signed two contracts.
Negativehave not / has not + p.p. + yetThe samples have not arrived yet.
QuestionHave / Has + subject + p.p.?Have you finished the report?
Linking wordshowever / therefore / in addition / as a resultThere is a delay; however, we are on track.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use already para algo concluído antes do esperado e yet para algo ainda não feito (em frases negativas e perguntas). Ligue suas ideias: however (porém), therefore (portanto), in addition (além disso), as a result (como resultado).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We have already our target for the quarter."
"The samples have not arrived ."
"There has been a small on one order."
"There is a delay; , the project is on track."
"I want to one risk in the report."
"Here is a quick of the quarter."

Stage 2: Put the Update Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a good status update in the correct order.

?Then, report any problem, for example a delay or a pending order.
?First, give a short overview of the project so far.
?Finally, say what is still on track and when you will send the full report.
?Next, highlight the milestones you have reached and the targets you have achieved.
?After that, explain what you have done about it and the result.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on the clear "have" and "has".

We have made good progress this week.
Fizemos um bom progresso esta semana.
We have already reached an important milestone.
Já alcançamos um marco importante.
I have not finished the report yet.
Eu ainda não terminei o relatório.
There is a small delay; however, we are on track.
Há um pequeno atraso; porém, estamos no caminho certo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each reporting situation.

The director asks how the project is going. The best reply is:
A "We have made good progress, and the project is on track."
B "We make progress yesterday on track."
C "Project going, no problem maybe."
You need to report that one task is not done. You say:
A "I have already not finish it."
B "I have not finished it yet; however, it is almost ready."
C "It not finish until now still."
You want to connect a problem to its result. The best sentence is:
A "The samples are late; therefore, there has been a delay."
B "The samples are late, however delay there is."
C "Samples late so delay because result."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your director asks for a status update on a project at work. Out loud, give a short overview. Use the present perfect to say what you have already achieved and what you have not finished yet, highlight one problem such as a delay or a pending task, and use a linking word (however, therefore, in addition, or as a result) to connect your ideas.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1I have an update on the project for you.Tenho uma atualização do projeto para você.
2So far, we have made good progress.Até agora, fizemos um bom progresso.
3We have already achieved our main target.Já alcançamos nossa meta principal.
4There has been a small delay; however, we are on track.Houve um pequeno atraso; porém, estamos no caminho certo.
5I have not finished the report yet, but I will send it soon.Ainda não terminei o relatório, mas vou enviá-lo em breve.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Handling Phone Calls -- Phone Expressions and Indirect Questions

Making and taking business phone calls politely: phone expressions (hold on, put through, transfer, leave a message, call back) and indirect questions (Could you tell me...? Do you know if...? I was wondering if...?). New vocabulary: hold on, put through, transfer, extension, voicemail, get through, hang up, call back, available, leave a message, caller, speak up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Hold on -- to wait on the line without ending the call
"Could you hold on for a moment, please?" (Esperar na linha)
Put through -- to connect a caller to another person
"I will put you through to the sales team." (Conectar / passar a ligação)
Transfer -- to move a call to another person or department
"Let me transfer your call to logistics." (Transferir a ligação)
Extension -- an internal phone number inside a company
"You can reach me on extension two-oh-four." (Ramal)
Voicemail -- a system that records phone messages
"Please leave a message on my voicemail." (Correio de voz)
Get through -- to succeed in reaching someone by phone
"I could not get through to the office this morning." (Conseguir falar / completar a ligação)
Hang up -- to end a phone call
"Please do not hang up; I will be right back." (Desligar)
Call back -- to return a phone call later
"I will call you back this afternoon." (Retornar a ligação)
Available -- free and able to talk now
"She is not available at the moment." (Disponível)
Leave a message -- to record information for someone who is away
"Would you like to leave a message?" (Deixar um recado)
Caller -- the person who is making the phone call
"Could you tell me who the caller is?" (Quem liga / interlocutor)
Speak up -- to talk more loudly so people can hear you
"Could you speak up, please? The line is not clear." (Falar mais alto)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Hold on
Put through
Transfer
Extension
Voicemail
Get through
Hang up
Call back
Available
Leave a message
Caller
Speak up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson needs to call a supplier about a pending order. First, he calls the main office. The receptionist answers and says the manager is not available right now. Walyson stays polite and uses indirect questions. He says, Could you tell me when the manager will be back? and Do you know if he has read my email? The receptionist offers to put him through to the assistant, or to take a message. Walyson asks her to hold on for a moment while he finds the order number. In the end, he leaves his extension and asks the assistant to call him back. Before he hangs up, he says, I was wondering if you could confirm the delivery date. A clear, polite call saves a lot of time.

1. Why does Walyson call the supplier?
A Because he needs to ask about a pending order.
B Because he wants to cancel everything.
C Because he is on holiday.
2. What does the receptionist say about the manager?
A He has already left the company.
B He is not available right now.
C He will never call back.
3. What does Walyson ask the assistant to do?
A To hang up immediately.
B To change the order.
C To call him back and confirm the delivery date.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Usamos perguntas indiretas para soar mais educados ao telefone. / We use indirect questions to sound more polite on the phone.

Form / FormaStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Direct questionWh- + auxiliary + subject?When will the order arrive?
Indirect (Wh-)Could you tell me + Wh- + subject + verbCould you tell me when the order will arrive?
Yes/No questionDo you know + if/whether + subject + verbDo you know if she is available?
Polite openerI was wondering if + subject + couldI was wondering if you could help me.

Key point / Ponto-chave: em perguntas indiretas, use a ordem de uma frase afirmativa (sujeito + verbo), sem do/does/did, e use if ou whether para perguntas de sim/não. Diga "Could you tell me where the office is?", não "where is the office?".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you tell me if the manager is ?"
"Would you like to leave a ?"
"Please on while I transfer your call."
"I will you back this afternoon."
"You can reach me on two-oh-four."
"I could not get to the office this morning."

Stage 2: Put the Call Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a professional phone call in the correct order.

?Then, if the person they want is not available, offer to take a message.
?First, answer the call and say your name and your company.
?Finally, confirm the next step and say a polite goodbye.
?Next, ask politely who is calling and how you can help.
?After that, use an indirect question to check the important details.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Keep a calm, polite phone voice.

Could you tell me if she is available?
Você poderia me dizer se ela está disponível?
I was wondering if you could call back later.
Eu queria saber se você poderia retornar a ligação mais tarde.
Please hold on while I put you through.
Aguarde na linha enquanto eu transfiro a ligação.
I could not get through, so I left a message.
Não consegui completar a ligação, então deixei um recado.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each phone situation.

A caller asks for your manager, who is busy. The best reply is:
A "He is not available right now. Would you like to leave a message?"
B "He no can talk. Bye."
C "Manager busy, you call other day maybe."
You want to ask politely when the order will arrive. You say:
A "Could you tell me when will arrive the order?"
B "Could you tell me when the order will arrive?"
C "When the order arrive you tell me?"
The line is bad and you cannot hear well. You say:
A "Could you speak up, please? The line is not clear."
B "You talk bad, more strong now."
C "I no listen, hang up yes?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you answer a business call and the person they want is not available. Out loud, handle the call: say who you are, say the person is not available, offer to take a message or to put the caller through, and ask one detail with an indirect question (for example, Could you tell me...? or Do you know if...?). End the call politely.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Could you tell me if she is available?Você poderia me dizer se ela está disponível?
2Would you like to leave a message?Você gostaria de deixar um recado?
3Please hold on while I transfer your call.Aguarde na linha enquanto eu transfiro sua ligação.
4I will call you back this afternoon.Eu vou retornar a ligação hoje à tarde.
5Could you speak up, please? The line is not clear.Você poderia falar mais alto, por favor? A linha não está boa.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Looking Back, Moving Forward -- Review and Self-Assessment

Reviewing your first block and assessing your progress: reflection vocabulary (strength, weakness, improve, goal, feedback) and the grammar of used to and would to compare the past with now. New vocabulary: strength, weakness, improve, struggle, confident, fluent, review, assess, goal, feedback, overcome, reflect.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Strength -- a thing you are good at; a strong point
"My biggest strength is writing clear emails." (Ponto forte)
Weakness -- a thing you are not good at yet; a weak point
"My main weakness is speaking too quickly." (Ponto fraco)
Improve -- to get better at something over time
"I want to improve my speaking this year." (Melhorar)
Struggle -- to find something hard and try with effort
"I still struggle with the past tense." (Ter dificuldade)
Confident -- feeling sure about yourself and your skills
"Now I feel confident on the phone." (Confiante)
Fluent -- able to speak a language smoothly and easily
"I speak more fluently than before." (Fluente)
Review -- to look at or study something again
"I review one lesson every week." (Revisar)
Assess -- to judge or measure how good something is
"Today you assess your own progress." (Avaliar)
Goal -- something you want to reach or achieve
"My goal is to speak in longer sentences." (Meta)
Feedback -- helpful comments about your work or progress
"The feedback from my teacher helped me a lot." (Retorno / feedback)
Overcome -- to deal with and beat a problem or fear
"I want to overcome my fear of mistakes." (Superar)
Reflect -- to think carefully about your experience
"I reflect on my progress every month." (Refletir)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Strength
Weakness
Improve
Struggle
Confident
Fluent
Review
Assess
Goal
Feedback
Overcome
Reflect

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After eight lessons, Walyson stops to reflect on his progress. He remembers that he used to feel nervous in every meeting, and that he would avoid the phone in English. Now he feels much more confident. He is honest about his learning. His main strength is writing clear emails, but his main weakness is speaking quickly, so he still wants to improve his fluency. His teacher gives him simple feedback: review a little every week and set one clear goal. Walyson decides to assess himself honestly and to overcome his fear of mistakes. A short, honest reflection helps him grow.

1. How did Walyson use to feel?
A He used to feel nervous and would avoid the phone in English.
B He used to feel completely relaxed in every meeting.
C He never studied English before.
2. What is his main strength and his main weakness?
A His strength is grammar; his weakness is reading.
B His strength is writing emails; his weakness is speaking quickly.
C He has no strengths and no weaknesses.
3. What feedback does the teacher give?
A To stop studying for a while.
B To review a little every week and set one clear goal.
C To change his teacher.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- used to / would

GRAMMAR

Usamos used to e would para comparar o passado com o presente. / We use used to and would to compare the past with now.

Form / FormaStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Past habit / stateused to + base verbI used to feel nervous.
Past repeated actionwould + base verbI would practice every morning.
Negativedid not + use to + base verbI did not use to feel confident.
Now (present)present simpleNow I feel confident.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use used to + verbo base para hábitos e estados do passado que mudaram. Use would apenas para ações repetidas, não para estados (diga "I used to be", não "I would be"). No negativo e na pergunta, tire o -d: use to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"My biggest is writing emails."
"My main is speaking too quickly."
"I want to my speaking."
"My is to be more fluent."
"The from my teacher helped me."
"I want to my fear of mistakes."

Stage 2: Put the Self-Assessment Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a good self-assessment in the correct order.

?After that, be honest about your weaknesses.
?First, look back at the lessons you have studied so far.
?Finally, set a clear goal for the next block.
?Next, write down your main strengths.
?Then, ask your teacher for feedback.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

I used to feel nervous, but now I feel confident.
Eu costumava ficar nervoso, mas agora me sinto confiante.
My main strength is writing clear emails.
Meu principal ponto forte é escrever e-mails claros.
I still struggle with speaking quickly.
Eu ainda tenho dificuldade em falar rapidamente.
My goal is to overcome my fear of mistakes.
Minha meta é superar meu medo de errar.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to say a past habit that has changed. You say:
A "I used to translate every word, but now I think in English."
B "I use to translate every word."
C "I am translate every word before."
You want to describe a past state, not an action. You say:
A "I would be shy in meetings."
B "I used to be shy in meetings."
C "I am used be shy in meetings."
You want to set a clear goal. You say:
A "My goal is improve my speaking."
B "My goal is to improve my speaking and overcome my fear."
C "My goal speaking improve fear."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record your own self-assessment journal out loud. Say how you used to speak English when you started, how you speak now, your main strength, your main weakness, and one clear goal for the next block. Use used to, would, and at least three of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1I used to feel nervous, but now I feel confident.Eu costumava ficar nervoso, mas agora me sinto confiante.
2My biggest strength is writing emails.Meu maior ponto forte é escrever e-mails.
3I still struggle with speaking quickly.Eu ainda tenho dificuldade em falar rapidamente.
4My goal is to improve my fluency.Minha meta é melhorar minha fluência.
5The feedback helped me overcome my fear of mistakes.O retorno me ajudou a superar meu medo de errar.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Meetings That Matter -- Running a Team Meeting

Learning to run a team meeting in English: meeting vocabulary (agenda, minutes, attendee, recap, action item) and the modal verbs should, could and might to make suggestions and recommendations. New vocabulary: agenda, minutes, attendee, chair, recap, action item, postpone, allocate, brainstorm, proposal, consensus, adjourn.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agenda -- the list of topics to discuss in a meeting
"Let us follow the agenda for today." (Pauta)
Minutes -- the written record of a meeting
"I will take the minutes today." (Ata)
Attendee -- a person who takes part in a meeting
"Every attendee should read the agenda." (Participante)
Chair -- to lead a meeting and guide the discussion
"Walyson will chair the meeting." (Presidir)
Recap -- a short summary of the main points
"Let me recap the main decisions." (Recapitular)
Action item -- a task someone must do after the meeting
"Each action item has one owner." (Tarefa)
Postpone -- to move an event to a later time or date
"We might postpone the launch." (Adiar)
Allocate -- to give money, time or people to a task
"We should allocate the budget carefully." (Alocar)
Brainstorm -- to share many ideas quickly as a group
"Let us brainstorm ideas for the launch." (Gerar ideias)
Proposal -- a plan or idea you suggest for a decision
"Maria shared a strong proposal." (Proposta)
Consensus -- general agreement among all the people
"We need consensus before we decide." (Consenso)
Adjourn -- to close a meeting, often until a later time
"We could adjourn before lunch." (Encerrar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agenda
Minutes
Attendee
Chair
Recap
Action item
Postpone
Allocate
Brainstorm
Proposal
Consensus
Adjourn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Today Walyson will chair the project meeting for the first time. He sends the agenda to every attendee in advance. He thinks they should start with the budget, because the team needs to allocate the money carefully. After that, they could brainstorm ideas for the launch and review Maria's proposal. The client might join for the last ten minutes, so Walyson will be ready to recap the decisions. If the team reaches consensus, they could adjourn before lunch. Walyson will take the minutes and allocate each action item to one owner.

1. What should the team start with, and why?
A The budget, because they need to allocate the money carefully.
B Lunch, because everyone is hungry.
C Nothing, because there is no agenda.
2. Why does Walyson say the client "might" join, not "will" join?
A Because it is certain the client will come.
B Because it is only a possibility, not certain.
C Because the client never comes to meetings.
3. When could the team adjourn before lunch?
A When the client arrives.
C When Walyson takes the minutes.
B If the team reaches consensus.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- should / could / might

GRAMMAR

Usamos should, could e might para fazer sugestões e recomendações. / We use should, could and might to make suggestions and recommendations.

ModalUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
shouldadvice / recommendation (forte)We should start on time.
couldsuggestion / option (mais leve)We could postpone the launch.
mightpossibility (incerta)The client might join late.
Negativemodal + notWe should not be late.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de should, could e might vem o verbo base, sem to e sem -s. Diga "he should start", não "he should starts" nem "he should to start". Use should para conselho forte, could para uma opção mais leve e might para algo incerto.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let us follow the for today."
"We start with the budget."
"We brainstorm ideas for the launch."
"The client join us later."
"Let me the main decisions."
"If we reach consensus, we could early."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Meeting in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a good meeting in the correct order.

?After that, discuss each point and brainstorm ideas.
?First, share the agenda with every attendee.
?Finally, recap the decisions and adjourn the meeting.
?Next, start the meeting and take the minutes.
?Then, allocate the action items to their owners.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Let us follow the agenda and start with the budget.
Vamos seguir a pauta e começar pelo orçamento.
We could brainstorm ideas before we decide.
Poderíamos gerar ideias antes de decidir.
The client might join for the last ten minutes.
O cliente talvez entre nos últimos dez minutos.
Let me recap the action items, and then we adjourn.
Deixe-me recapitular as tarefas e então encerramos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to give strong advice to start now. You say:
A "We should start now and follow the agenda."
B "We should to start now."
C "We should starts now."
You want to suggest a possible option, not an order. You say:
A "We must postpone the launch."
B "We could postpone the launch by two weeks."
C "We could to postpone the launch."
You are not sure if the client will come. You say:
A "The client will definitely join."
B "The client might join for the last ten minutes."
C "The client might joins for ten minutes."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you chair a short team meeting. Open with the agenda, make three suggestions using should, could and might, allocate one action item, and recap before you adjourn. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1Let us follow the agenda and start with the budget.Vamos seguir a pauta e começar pelo orçamento.
2We could brainstorm ideas before we decide.Poderíamos gerar ideias antes de decidir.
3The client might join for the last ten minutes.O cliente talvez entre nos últimos dez minutos.
4Let me recap the action items for everyone.Deixe-me recapitular as tarefas para todos.
5If we reach consensus, we could adjourn early.Se chegarmos a um consenso, poderíamos encerrar mais cedo.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Agreeing and Disagreeing -- Debating a Business Decision

Learning to agree and disagree in a diplomatic way at work: the language of opinions, softeners and polite pushback to debate a business decision. New vocabulary: point of view, agree with, disagree, softener, push back, concede, valid, on the same page, see your point, fair enough, compromise, raise a concern.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Point of view -- the way a person thinks about something
"Let me share my point of view." (Ponto de vista)
Agree with -- to have the same opinion as someone
"I agree with your idea." (Concordar com)
Disagree -- to have a different opinion
"I disagree, but I respect your point." (Discordar)
Softener -- a polite phrase that makes your words gentler
"Use a softener before you disagree." (Amenizador)
Push back -- to politely say no or argue against an idea
"I have to push back on the deadline." (Contestar)
Concede -- to accept that the other person is partly right
"I concede that the price is good." (Reconhecer/Ceder)
Valid -- reasonable and worth considering
"That is a valid concern." (Válido)
On the same page -- in full agreement about something
"Are we on the same page now?" (De acordo)
See your point -- to understand the other person's reason
"I see your point about quality." (Entendo seu ponto)
Fair enough -- a phrase to accept the other person's idea politely
"Fair enough, let us try that." (Faz sentido / Justo)
Compromise -- a solution where each side gives up a little
"Let us find a compromise." (Meio-termo / Acordo)
Raise a concern -- to mention a worry or a possible problem
"I want to raise a concern about the cost." (Levantar uma preocupação)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Point of view
Agree with
Disagree
Softener
Push back
Concede
Valid
On the same page
See your point
Fair enough
Compromise
Raise a concern

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In the debate about the new coffee supplier, Walyson shares his point of view calmly. He agrees with Sofia on the price, and he says it is a valid option. But he wants to raise a concern about quality, so he uses a softener first: "I see your point, but I am not sure about the delivery time." He does not just disagree; he uses a polite phrase to push back. Sofia answers, "Fair enough." She is happy to concede that quality matters. They are not on the same page at first, but in the end they find a compromise and test the supplier with a small order.

1. What does Walyson agree with Sofia about?
A The price, which he calls a valid option.
B The quality of the coffee.
C Nothing at all.
2. Why does Walyson use a softener before he disagrees?
A To win the argument quickly.
B To push back politely without sounding rude.
C Because he agrees with everything.
3. How do Walyson and Sofia end the debate?
A They cancel the order completely.
C They never agree on anything.
B They find a compromise and test the supplier with a small order.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Softeners for diplomatic disagreement

GRAMMAR

Usamos amenizadores (softeners) antes de discordar, para soar educado e profissional. / We use softeners before we disagree, to sound polite and professional.

Function / FunçãoPhrase / FraseUse / Uso
AgreeI agree with you. / You are right.concordar diretamente
SoftenI see your point, but... / That is valid, however...reconhecer antes de discordar
DisagreeI am not sure I agree. / I have to push back on that.discordar com educação
ResolveFair enough. / Let us find a compromise.fechar com um acordo

Key point / Ponto-chave: em inglês profissional, raramente dizemos só "No, you are wrong". Primeiro use um softener ("I see your point, but..."), depois push back com um motivo claro, e termine buscando um compromise. Isso mantém o respeito e ajuda a chegar a um acordo. / In professional English, we rarely just say "No, you are wrong."
