First use a softener, then push back with a clear reason, and end by looking for a compromise.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me share my on this."
"I you on the price."
"I see your point, but I have to on the deadline."
"That is a concern about quality."
", let us try your idea."
"We do not fully agree, so let us find a ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of Diplomatic Disagreement in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a polite disagreement in the correct order.

?After that, use a softener before you push back.
?First, listen and show you understand their point of view.
?Finally, suggest a compromise to move forward together.
?Next, agree with the part that is valid.
?Then, give a clear reason for your own opinion.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

I see your point, but I have to push back a little.
Entendo seu ponto, mas preciso contestar um pouco.
That is a valid concern, and I agree with part of it.
É uma preocupação válida, e concordo com parte dela.
Fair enough, but I am not sure we are on the same page.
Justo, mas não tenho certeza de que estamos de acordo.
Let us find a compromise that works for both teams.
Vamos encontrar um meio-termo que funcione para os dois times.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A colleague suggests a supplier you do not like. You want to disagree politely. You say:
A "I see your point, but I have to push back on the quality."
B "No, you are completely wrong."
C "I do not care about your idea."
You think your colleague's worry about cost is reasonable. You say:
A "That concern is not important."
B "That is a valid concern, and I agree with you."
C "We must do it my way."
The team cannot fully agree. You want to suggest a middle solution. You say:
A "Let us cancel everything."
B "Let us find a compromise we can both accept."
C "I disagree, end of discussion."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a colleague suggests a decision you partly disagree with at Sucafina. Share your point of view: agree with one valid part, use a softener, push back with a clear reason, and suggest a compromise. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1I see your point, but I have to push back a little.Entendo seu ponto, mas preciso contestar um pouco.
2That is a valid concern, and I agree with part of it.É uma preocupação válida, e concordo com parte dela.
3Fair enough, but I am not sure we are on the same page.Justo, mas não tenho certeza de que estamos de acordo.
4Let me share my point of view before we decide.Deixe-me compartilhar meu ponto de vista antes de decidirmos.
5Let us find a compromise that works for both teams.Vamos encontrar um meio-termo que funcione para os dois times.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Making Presentations -- The Coffee Market Briefing

Learning to deliver a clear five-minute presentation with signposting language: opening with an outline, sequencing points with first, then and finally, walking the audience through a visual aid, and summing up the key takeaway. New vocabulary: outline, audience, signpost, slide deck, walk through, move on, emphasize, visual aid, sum up, key takeaway, handout, Q and A.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Outline -- a short plan that lists the main parts of a talk
"Let me give you the outline of my talk." (Estrutura / Roteiro)
Audience -- the group of people who listen to your talk
"I want to engage the audience from the start." (Plateia / Público)
Signpost -- a phrase that shows where the talk is going
"First, then and finally are signposts." (Sinalizador / Marcador)
Slide deck -- the full set of slides you use in a talk
"My slide deck has only five slides." (Conjunto de slides)
Walk through -- to explain something step by step
"Let me walk you through this chart." (Explicar passo a passo)
Move on -- to leave one point and go to the next
"Let us move on to the prices." (Seguir em frente / Passar para)
Emphasize -- to make a point stronger so people remember it
"I want to emphasize the strong harvest." (Enfatizar / Destacar)
Visual aid -- a chart or image that helps the audience
"This chart is my main visual aid." (Recurso visual)
Sum up -- to repeat the main message shortly at the end
"Let me sum up the three points." (Resumir)
Key takeaway -- the one main idea you want people to remember
"The key takeaway is the strong harvest." (Mensagem principal)
Handout -- a paper with information you give to the audience
"There is a handout with the numbers." (Material impresso)
Q and A -- the questions and answers part after a talk
"We will have a short Q and A at the end." (Sessão de perguntas)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Outline
Audience
Signpost
Slide deck
Walk through
Move on
Emphasize
Visual aid
Sum up
Key takeaway
Q and A

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson prepares a short briefing for the trading team. He starts with the outline, so the audience knows his plan: three points in five minutes. First, he presents the harvest and uses a clear signpost before each point. Then, he says "let us move on to the prices" and uses one chart as his main visual aid. He decides to walk the team through the numbers slowly and to emphasize the strong volume. Finally, he wants to sum up the key takeaway in one sentence and open a short Q and A. His goal is simple: a clear talk where the audience always knows where he is.

1. Why does Walyson start with the outline?
A So the audience knows his plan from the start.
B To make the talk longer.
C To surprise the audience at the end.
2. How does Walyson move between his points?
A He jumps with no warning.
B He uses signposts like first, then and finally.
C He reads every slide word by word.
3. What does Walyson do at the end?
A He stops with no conclusion.
C He starts a new presentation.
B He sums up the key takeaway and opens a Q and A.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Signposting language for presentations

GRAMMAR

Usamos sinalizadores (signposts) para guiar a plateia por cada parte da apresentação. / We use signposts to guide the audience through each part of the presentation.

Stage / EtapaPhrase / FraseUse / Uso
OpenI have divided my talk into three parts.dar a estrutura (outline)
StartFirst, I will give you an overview.abrir o primeiro ponto
Move onThen / After that, let us move on to...mudar de ponto
CloseFinally, to sum up... Any questions?resumir e abrir o Q and A

Key point / Ponto-chave: os signposts são placas de sinalização para a plateia. Abra com o outline, comece com first, mude de ponto com then ou after that, e feche com finally antes de sum up. Assim a plateia sempre sabe onde você está. / Signposts are road signs for the audience. Open with the outline, start with first, change points with then or after that, and close with finally before you sum up.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me give you the of my talk."
"Then, let us to the prices."
"Let me this chart."
"This chart is my main ."
"Finally, let me the key takeaway."
"We will have a short at the end."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Clear Presentation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a clear presentation in the correct order.

?After that, move on to the second point and walk the audience through your visual aid.
?First, open with a clear outline, so the audience knows your plan.
?Finally, sum up your message and open the floor for a short Q and A.
?Next, signpost the first point and present it with one simple slide.
?Then, emphasize your key takeaway, so people remember it.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Let me give you the outline. I have divided my talk into three parts.
Deixe-me dar a estrutura. Dividi minha apresentação em três partes.
First, I will walk you through the harvest numbers.
Primeiro, vou explicar passo a passo os números da safra.
Then, let us move on to the prices and demand.
Então, vamos passar para os preços e a demanda.
Finally, let me sum up the key takeaway.
Por fim, deixe-me resumir a mensagem principal.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You start your briefing and want to tell the team your plan. You say:
A "Let me give you the outline. I have divided my talk into three parts."
B "I will just talk and you will see."
C "There is no plan today."
You finished the first point and want to go to the prices. You say:
A "Now random the prices thing."
B "Then, let us move on to the prices."
C "Forget the first point completely."
You reach the end of the talk. You want to close and invite questions. You say:
A "That is all, bye."
B "Finally, let me sum up the key takeaway. Now a short Q and A."
C "No questions are allowed."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you present a short update at Sucafina. Give the outline, signpost three points with first, then and finally, walk the audience through one visual aid, and sum up the key takeaway. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Let me give you the outline. I have divided my talk into three parts.Deixe-me dar a estrutura. Dividi minha apresentação em três partes.
2First, I will walk you through the harvest numbers.Primeiro, vou explicar passo a passo os números da safra.
3Then, let us move on to the prices and demand.Então, vamos passar para os preços e a demanda.
4Finally, let me sum up the key takeaway.Por fim, deixe-me resumir a mensagem principal.
5Now we will have a short Q and A. Any questions?Agora teremos uma breve sessão de perguntas. Alguma pergunta?
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Negotiating a Deal -- The Santos Coffee Contract

Learning to bargain on price and terms with conditional language: making offers with if, unless and provided, stating your bottom line, offering a trade-off, naming a deal-breaker, and meeting halfway to close a deal in good faith. New vocabulary: bottom line, leverage, counteroffer, trade-off, terms, meet halfway, walk away, deadlock, leeway, ballpark, deal-breaker, in good faith.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Bottom line -- the limit you cannot pass, like the lowest price
"My bottom line is the floor price from the mill." (Limite mínimo)
Leverage -- an advantage that gives your side power
"Our strong harvest is our main leverage." (Poder de barganha / Vantagem)
Counteroffer -- a new offer you give back after their offer
"They asked for ten percent; my counteroffer was three." (Contraproposta)
Trade-off -- give up one thing to gain another in a deal
"A discount for a bigger order is a fair trade-off." (Troca / Concessão mútua)
Terms -- the conditions of a deal: price, volume, dates
"Let us agree on the terms of the contract." (Condições / Termos)
Meet halfway -- both sides give a little to reach an agreement
"Let us meet halfway and split the difference." (Chegar a um meio-termo)
Walk away -- to leave a deal that is not good for you
"If the price is too high, I will walk away." (Desistir / Sair da negociação)
Deadlock -- a point where neither side will move
"We reached a deadlock on the delivery date." (Impasse)
Leeway -- some freedom or room to move on a condition
"We have some leeway on the volume, not the price." (Margem / Folga)
Ballpark -- an approximate number, not the exact one
"Give me a ballpark price and we will refine it." (Valor aproximado / Estimativa)
Deal-breaker -- a condition that, if not met, ends the deal
"A late shipment is a deal-breaker for us." (Fator decisivo / Ponto inegociável)
In good faith -- honestly and fairly, with a real wish to agree
"We want to negotiate in good faith." (De boa-fé)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Bottom line
Leverage
Counteroffer
Trade-off
Terms
Meet halfway
Walk away
Deadlock
Leeway
Ballpark
Deal-breaker

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson prepares for a negotiation with a buyer. He knows his bottom line: he cannot go below the floor price from the mill. His leverage is the strong harvest from Santos. The buyer asks for a big discount, but Walyson does not just say yes. If they increase the volume, he will give a small discount: a fair trade-off. Unless the buyer confirms a firm delivery date, he will not lower the price, because a late shipment is a deal-breaker. He has some leeway on the volume, so provided they pay on time, he can guarantee an early shipment. His goal is to negotiate in good faith and meet halfway to close the deal.

1. What will Walyson do if the buyer increases the volume?
A He will give a small discount as a trade-off.
B He will raise the price.
C He will walk away immediately.
2. What is Walyson's deal-breaker?
A A bigger order.
B A late shipment.
C A small discount.
3. On what condition can Walyson guarantee an early shipment?
A Only if the buyer pays less.
C Only if he raises the price.
B Provided the buyer pays on time.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditional language (if / unless / provided)

GRAMMAR

Usamos a linguagem condicional para negociar: ofereço algo SE você me der algo em troca. / We use conditional language to negotiate: I offer something IF you give me something in return.

Word / PalavraPhrase / FraseUse / Uso
IfIf you order more, I will lower the price.condição normal (se)
UnlessUnless you confirm the date, I cannot offer it.a não ser que / se não (= if not)
ProvidedProvided you pay on time, we will give a discount.desde que (só nesta condição)

Key point / Ponto-chave: a parte da condição usa o present simple (you order, you pay) e a parte do resultado usa will ou can. Nunca use will depois de if, unless ou provided. / The condition clause uses the present simple and the result clause uses will or can. Never use will after if, unless or provided.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" you order more, I will lower the price."
" you confirm the date, I cannot offer a discount."
" you pay on time, we will give a discount."
"My is the floor price from the mill."
"A late shipment is a for us."
"Let us and close the deal."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Negotiation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a negotiation in the correct order.

?After that, listen to their position and look for a trade-off you can both accept.
?First, prepare your bottom line and know your leverage before the meeting.
?Finally, meet halfway and agree on the terms, or walk away if the deal does not work.
?Next, open in good faith and give the other side a ballpark number.
?Then, if they push for more, offer a small concession provided they give you something in return.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

If you increase the volume, I will give you a small discount.
Se você aumentar o volume, eu darei um pequeno desconto.
Unless you confirm the date, I cannot offer a discount.
A não ser que você confirme a data, não posso oferecer desconto.
Provided you pay on time, we will give a discount.
Desde que você pague em dia, daremos um desconto.
Let us meet halfway and close the deal in good faith.
Vamos chegar a um meio-termo e fechar o acordo de boa-fé.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

The buyer wants a discount, but only if they order more. You say:
A "If you increase the volume, I will give you a small discount."
B "I will give you everything for free."
C "There is no discount, ever."
You cannot move on price until they confirm the delivery date. You say:
A "If you will confirm the date, I lower the price."
B "Unless you confirm the date, I cannot offer a discount."
C "The date is not important at all."
The two sides are close and you want to close the deal fairly. You say:
A "Take it or leave it now."
B "Let us meet halfway and close the deal in good faith."
C "I will keep everything for my side."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you negotiate a coffee contract at Sucafina. State your bottom line, make a conditional offer with if and provided, name a deal-breaker, and meet halfway to close the deal. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1If you increase the volume, I will give you a small discount.Se você aumentar o volume, eu darei um pequeno desconto.
2Unless you confirm the date, I cannot offer a discount.A não ser que você confirme a data, não posso oferecer desconto.
3Provided you pay on time, we will give a discount.Desde que você pague em dia, daremos um desconto.
4My bottom line is the floor price; that is a fair trade-off.Meu limite mínimo é o preço de base; isso é uma troca justa.
5Let us meet halfway and close the deal in good faith.Vamos chegar a um meio-termo e fechar o acordo de boa-fé.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Reading Contracts -- The Santos Coffee Contract

Learning to read a contract: finding clauses and obligations, and using shall, must and be required to to express a duty. Reading the obligation, warranty and liability clauses, the effective date, the expiry date and the governing law, and knowing when a contract is binding. New vocabulary: clause, party, obligation, liability, breach, comply with, binding, warranty, indemnify, effective date, expiry date, governing law.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Clause -- a separate, numbered part of a contract with one rule
"The delivery clause says the coffee ships by the tenth." (Cláusula)
Party -- a person or company that signs and is bound by a contract
"Each party must sign the last page of the contract." (Parte / Contratante)
Obligation -- a duty: something a party must do under the contract
"The seller's obligation is to deliver on time." (Obrigação / Dever)
Liability -- legal responsibility for a loss, damage or cost
"The liability clause limits the seller's responsibility." (Responsabilidade legal)
Breach -- a failure to do what the contract requires; a broken rule
"A late shipment would be a breach of the contract." (Quebra / Violação de contrato)
Comply with -- to act exactly as a rule or contract requires
"Both parties must comply with the delivery clause." (Cumprir / Obedecer)
Binding -- legally firm: once signed, both parties must follow it
"The contract is binding once both parties sign." (Vinculante / Obrigatório)
Warranty -- a written promise that a product meets a stated quality
"The warranty confirms the coffee is premium grade." (Garantia)
Indemnify -- to pay for the loss or damage another party suffers
"The party in breach must indemnify the other side." (Indenizar / Ressarcir)
Effective date -- the date when the contract begins to apply
"The seller shall deliver by the effective date." (Data de vigência / Início)
Expiry date -- the date when the contract ends and stops applying
"Check the expiry date before you renew the deal." (Data de término / Validade)
Governing law -- the country whose law decides any dispute in the contract
"The governing law of this contract is Brazilian law." (Lei aplicável / Foro)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Clause
Party
Obligation
Liability
Breach
Comply with
Binding
Warranty
Indemnify
Effective date
Expiry date
Governing law

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Walyson reads the Santos coffee contract before he signs it. Each party has duties written in numbered clauses. Under the obligation clause, the seller shall deliver the coffee by the effective date, and the buyer must pay within thirty days. The warranty clause promises that the coffee is premium grade. The liability clause is important: if a party is in breach, that party is required to indemnify the other side for any loss. Both parties must comply with every clause. The governing law is Brazilian law, and the contract is binding from the effective date until the expiry date.

1. What does the seller shall do under the obligation clause?
A Deliver the coffee by the effective date.
B Pay within thirty days.
C Change the governing law.
2. What must a party in breach do?
A Sign the contract again.
B Indemnify the other side for any loss.
C Lower the price.
3. When is the contract binding?
A Only after the expiry date.
C Only if the buyer asks for a discount.
B From the effective date, once both parties sign.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Obligation language (shall / must / be required to)

GRAMMAR

Em contratos, a obrigação se escreve com palavras especiais: shall, must e be required to. Todas significam um dever forte. / In contracts, an obligation is written with special words: shall, must and be required to. They all mean a strong duty.

Word / PalavraPhrase / FraseUse / Uso
shallThe seller shall deliver the coffee.muito formal, típico de contrato (deverá / deve)
mustThe buyer must pay within thirty days.regra forte, também na fala (deve / tem que)
be required toEach party is required to comply.formal (= must) (é obrigado a)

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de shall e must usamos o verbo base (shall deliver, must pay) -- NUNCA "to + verbo". Só be required to mantém o "to". Para proibição, use shall not ou must not. / After shall and must we use the base verb -- never "to + verb". Only be required to keeps the "to". For a prohibition, use shall not or must not.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The seller deliver the coffee by the effective date."
"The buyer pay within thirty days."
"A party in breach must the other side."
"Both parties must every clause."
"The contract is once both parties sign."
"The of this contract is Brazilian law."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of Reading a Contract in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of reading a contract in the correct order.

?After that, read the warranty and the liability clause to know who is responsible if there is a breach.
?First, read the whole contract slowly and find the main clauses.
?Finally, ask the legal team about any clause that is not clear, and only sign when the contract is correct and binding.
?Next, check the obligation clause to see what each party shall and must do.
?Then, confirm the effective date, the expiry date, and the governing law.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The seller shall deliver the coffee by the effective date.
O vendedor deverá entregar o café até a data de vigência.
The buyer must pay within thirty days.
O comprador deve pagar em até trinta dias.
A party in breach is required to indemnify the other side.
Uma parte em violação é obrigada a indenizar a outra parte.
The contract is binding once both parties sign.
O contrato é vinculante assim que ambas as partes assinam.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to write the seller's duty to deliver, in a formal contract. You write:
A "The seller shall deliver the coffee by the effective date."
B "The seller shall to deliver the coffee."
C "Maybe the seller delivers the coffee."
A party broke a rule of the contract. You describe what happened. You say:
A "That party met halfway on the contract."
B "That party is in breach and must indemnify the other side."
C "That party signed the warranty."
A colleague asks when the contract becomes firm for both sides. You answer:
A "It is binding only after the expiry date."
B "It is binding from the effective date, once both parties sign."
C "It is never binding."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you read a coffee contract at Sucafina and explain it to a colleague. Describe the obligation clause with shall and must, the liability clause with be required to and indemnify, and say when the contract is binding. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1The seller shall deliver the coffee by the effective date.O vendedor deverá entregar o café até a data de vigência.
2The buyer must pay within thirty days.O comprador deve pagar em até trinta dias.
3A party in breach is required to indemnify the other side.Uma parte em violação é obrigada a indenizar a outra parte.
4Both parties must comply with every clause.Ambas as partes devem cumprir cada cláusula.
5The contract is binding once both parties sign.O contrato é vinculante assim que ambas as partes assinam.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Writing a Formal Email -- Confirming the Coffee Contract Terms

Writing a polite, formal email to request and confirm contract terms in writing. Learning to open ("I am writing to confirm..."), make polite requests ("I would be grateful if you could...", "Would you kindly..."), enclose documents ("please find enclosed"), and close formally ("I look forward to..."). New vocabulary: enclose, kindly, further to, revised, amendment, clarification, await, look forward to, as per, acknowledge receipt, at your earliest convenience, sign-off.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Enclose -- to attach or include a document with your email or letter
"Please find enclosed the revised price table." (Anexar / Incluir)
Kindly -- a polite, formal way to say "please" in a request
"Would you kindly confirm the effective date?" (Por gentileza / Por favor)
Further to -- following up on; referring back to an earlier message or call
"Further to our last call, I am writing to confirm the terms." (Em referência a / Dando seguimento a)
Revised -- changed and corrected; a newer, updated version
"I attach the revised contract with the new price." (Revisado / Atualizado)
Amendment -- a formal change made to a clause of a contract
"Do you accept the amendment to clause two?" (Alteração / Emenda)
Clarification -- a clear answer that removes doubt about a point
"I need one clarification about the warranty." (Esclarecimento)
Await -- to wait for; a formal word used in business writing
"I await your written reply." (Aguardar)
Look forward to -- to feel happy about something that will happen; a polite closing
"I look forward to closing this deal with you." (Aguardar com expectativa / Estar ansioso por)
As per -- according to; in line with what was agreed
"As per our agreement, delivery is in the first week." (Conforme / De acordo com)
Acknowledge receipt -- to confirm in writing that you received a document
"I acknowledge receipt of the revised price table." (Confirmar o recebimento)
At your earliest convenience -- as soon as you can; a polite way to ask for a quick reply
"Please reply at your earliest convenience." (Assim que possível / Na primeira oportunidade)
Sign-off -- the polite closing line of an email, like "Best regards"
"A formal sign-off is 'Best regards, Walyson'." (Despedida / Fecho do e-mail)
Countersign -- to sign a document that the other party has already signed
"Kindly countersign the contract and send it back." (Assinar também / Contra-assinar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Enclose
Kindly
Further to
Revised
Amendment
Clarification
Await
Look forward to
As per
Acknowledge receipt
At your earliest convenience
Sign-off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the email and answer the questions.

Dear Clara,

Further to our last call, I am writing to confirm the coffee contract terms. I would be grateful if you could confirm the effective date and the delivery week. Please find enclosed the revised price table; I acknowledge receipt of your amendment to clause two and I accept it. Would you kindly send one clarification about the warranty? As per our agreement, I enclose the countersigned contract. I await your reply at your earliest convenience, and I look forward to working with you.

Best regards,
Walyson

1. Why is Walyson writing the email?
A To confirm the contract terms in writing.
B To cancel the contract.
C To ask for a new price only.
2. What does he do with the amendment to clause two?
A He rejects it.
B He acknowledges receipt of it and accepts it.
C He ignores it.
3. Which polite phrase does he use to make a request?
A "Send it now."
C "You have to confirm."
B "I would be grateful if you could confirm..."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite request and confirmation language

GRAMMAR

Em e-mails formais, pedimos e confirmamos com frases corteses, não com ordens diretas. / In formal emails, we request and confirm with polite phrases, not direct orders.

Function / FunçãoFormal phrase / Frase formalUse / Uso
Open / AbrirI am writing to confirm the terms.diz por que você escreve (escrevo para...)
Request / PedirI would be grateful if you could confirm... / Would you kindly send...pedido corts (eu agradeceria se / poderia, por gentileza)
Enclose / AnexarPlease find enclosed the price table.apresenta um anexo (segue em anexo)
Close / FecharI look forward to your reply. Best regards,despedida corts (aguardo / atenciosamente)

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de could, would e kindly usamos o verbo base (could confirm, would send). Após look forward to usamos -ing ou um substantivo (look forward to working / to your reply), pois aqui "to" é preposição. / After could, would and kindly we use the base verb. After look forward to we use -ing or a noun, because here "to" is a preposition.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Please find the revised price table."
"Would you confirm the effective date?"
" our last call, I am writing to confirm the terms."
"I of your amendment to clause two."
"I your reply at your earliest convenience."
"I working with you."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of Writing a Formal Email in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of writing a formal email in the correct order.

?After that, make your requests politely, using would you kindly or I would be grateful if you could.
?First, open the email with a formal greeting and a clear subject line.
?Finally, close with a polite sign-off, say you look forward to their reply, and proofread the email before you send it.
?Next, write one line that says why you are writing, for example I am writing to confirm the contract terms.
?Then, confirm the terms the other side asked about and say please find enclosed for any document.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

I am writing to confirm the contract terms.
Escrevo para confirmar os termos do contrato.
I would be grateful if you could confirm the effective date.
Eu agradeceria se você pudesse confirmar a data de vigência.
Please find enclosed the revised price table.
Segue em anexo a tabela de preços revisada.
I look forward to working with you.
Aguardo com expectativa trabalhar com você.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to ask Clara, politely and in writing, to confirm the delivery week. You write:
A "I would be grateful if you could confirm the delivery week."
B "Confirm the delivery week now."
C "Tell me the week, ok?"
You are attaching the revised contract to your email. You write:
A "I look forward to the contract."
B "Please find enclosed the revised contract."
C "The contract is a breach."
You want a quick but polite reply, and a friendly close. You write:
A "Answer fast. Bye."
B "I await your reply at your earliest convenience. I look forward to working with you."
C "Reply whenever, no problem."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you write a formal email to a buyer at Sucafina to confirm the coffee contract terms. Open with "I am writing to...", make one polite request with "would you kindly" or "I would be grateful if you could", enclose a document with "please find enclosed", and close with a polite sign-off and "I look forward to...". Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I am writing to confirm the contract terms.Escrevo para confirmar os termos do contrato.
2I would be grateful if you could confirm the effective date.Eu agradeceria se você pudesse confirmar a data de vigência.
3Please find enclosed the revised price table.Segue em anexo a tabela de preços revisada.
4Would you kindly countersign the contract?Poderia, por gentileza, contra-assinar o contrato?
5I look forward to working with you.Aguardo com expectativa trabalhar com você.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Negotiating by Email -- Offers and Counteroffers in Writing

Making and responding to written offers and counteroffers to negotiate the coffee price by email. Learning the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) to bargain politely about a deal that is not agreed yet. New vocabulary: offer, counterproposal, threshold, justify, sweeten, firm up, subject to, lock in, match, settle on, final offer, withdraw.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Offer -- a price or set of terms that you propose to the other side
"Here is my offer: three dollars forty per kilo." (Oferta / Proposta)
Counterproposal -- a different offer you send back when you do not accept theirs
"I do not accept the price, so here is my counterproposal." (Contraproposta)
Threshold -- a limit you cannot cross; the lowest price you can accept
"Three dollars twenty is below my threshold." (Limite / Piso)
Justify -- to give a clear reason that explains why your price is fair
"My cost has gone up, so I can justify the new price." (Justificar)
Sweeten -- to add something extra to make an offer more attractive
"If you matched the volume, we would sweeten the deal with free freight." (Adoçar / Tornar mais atraente)
Firm up -- to make an offer or order definite and final
"If she raised the price, I would firm up the order today." (Fechar / Confirmar em definitivo)
Subject to -- depending on a condition; only valid if that condition is met
"This offer is subject to the harvest and to my director's approval." (Sujeito a / Condicionado a)
Lock in -- to fix a price or rate so it cannot change later
"If you accepted today, we would lock in the rate for the season." (Travar / Fixar)
Match -- to offer the same price, volume or terms as the other side
"If you covered the freight, I would match your volume." (Igualar / Equiparar)
Settle on -- to agree, after bargaining, on a final price or figure
"In the end we settled on three dollars thirty-five." (Chegar a um acordo sobre / Fechar em)
Final offer -- the last price or terms you are willing to give in a negotiation
"Three dollars forty is my final offer." (Oferta final / Última oferta)
Withdraw -- to take back an offer that you made before
"I cannot withdraw the offer before Friday." (Retirar / Revogar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Offer
Counterproposal
Threshold
Justify
Sweeten
Firm up
Subject to
Lock in
Match
Settle on
Final offer
Withdraw

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the email and answer the questions.

Dear Clara,

Thank you for your offer. Three dollars twenty is below my threshold, and let me justify it: my cost has gone up. Here is my counterproposal: if you raised the price to three forty, I would firm up the order today. To sweeten it, if you covered the freight, I would match your volume and we would lock in the rate. This offer is subject to the harvest, and I cannot withdraw it before Friday. If we settled on three thirty-five, I would be happy. Three forty is my final offer.

Best regards,
Walyson

1. Why does Walyson not accept the offer of three dollars twenty?
A It is below his threshold, because his cost has gone up.
B He does not want the deal at all.
C The harvest already failed.
2. What would Walyson do if Clara raised the price to three forty?
A He would withdraw the offer.
B He would firm up the order today.
C He would lower his volume.
3. What figure would make Walyson happy?
A Three dollars twenty.
C Two dollars eighty.
B Three dollars thirty-five, if they settled on it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The second conditional

GRAMMAR

Usamos o second conditional para falar de um acordo imaginário, ainda não fechado, ao negociar. É mais educado que o first conditional. / We use the second conditional for an imaginary, not-yet-agreed deal when we bargain. It is more polite than the first conditional.

Part / ParteForm / FormaExample / Exemplo
If clause / Condiçãoif + past simpleIf you raised the price...
Result / Resultadowould + verbo base...I would firm up the order.
Meaning / Sentidoimaginário, não fechadooferta hipotética e educada

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de if use o passado simples (raised), e use would só no resultado, com o verbo base (would firm up). Compare com o first conditional (aula 13): "If you order more, I will give a discount" (real) vs. "If you ordered more, I would give a discount" (imaginário, mais educado). / After if use the past simple; use would only in the result, with the base verb.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If you the price, I would firm up the order today."
"If you covered the freight, I would your volume."
"Three dollars twenty is below my ."
"This offer is the harvest."
"If we on three thirty-five, I would be happy."
"Three dollars forty is my ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Negotiation Email in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of writing a negotiation email in the correct order.

?After that, make your counterproposal using the second conditional, for example if you raised the price, I would firm up the order.
?First, open the email and acknowledge the other side's offer politely.
?Finally, suggest a figure to settle on, set a deadline for the reply, and proofread the whole email before you send it.
?Next, justify your own position with a clear reason, for example that your cost has gone up.
?Then, add a condition to protect yourself, such as the offer is subject to the harvest or pending approval.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

If you raised the price, I would firm up the order today.
Se você aumentasse o preço, eu fecharia o pedido hoje.
That price is below my threshold.
Esse preço está abaixo do meu limite.
Let me sweeten the offer with free freight.
Deixe-me tornar a oferta mais atraente com frete grátis.
If we settled on three thirty-five, I would be happy.
Se fechássemos em três e trinta e cinco, eu ficaria satisfeito.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

The buyer's price is too low. You want to make a polite, imaginary counterproposal by email. You write:
A "If you raised the price, I would firm up the order today."
B "If you would raise the price, I will firm up the order."
C "Raise the price now."
You want to make your offer more attractive by adding free freight. You write:
A "I withdraw my offer."
B "Let me sweeten the offer: if you matched the volume, I would cover the freight."
C "The freight is your problem."
You want to protect your offer with a condition and a deadline. You write:
A "This price never changes."
B "This offer is subject to the harvest, and I cannot withdraw it after Friday."
C "Take it or leave it whenever."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a buyer at Sucafina emails you a low offer for your coffee. Write a short counterproposal email: acknowledge the offer, justify your price, make a counterproposal with the second conditional ("if you raised..., I would..."), sweeten it, add a condition with "subject to", and give your final offer. Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1If you raised the price, I would firm up the order.Se você aumentasse o preço, eu fecharia o pedido.
2That price is below my threshold.Esse preço está abaixo do meu limite.
3Let me sweeten the offer with free freight.Deixe-me tornar a oferta mais atraente com frete grátis.
4This offer is subject to the harvest.Esta oferta está sujeita à colheita.
5If we settled on three thirty-five, I would be happy.Se fechássemos em três e trinta e cinco, eu ficaria satisfeito.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Closing the Deal -- Confirming the Final Agreement in Writing

Confirming the final agreement and the next steps in writing after a negotiation. Learning reported speech (say / tell + backshift: is→was, will→would, can→could) to report what was decided. New vocabulary: agreement, as agreed, finalize, proceed, next steps, deliverable, timeline, outstanding, go-ahead, move forward, summary, on board.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agreement -- a deal that both sides have accepted on the terms
"We have reached an agreement on the price and volume." (Acordo)
As agreed -- a phrase that repeats what both sides already decided
"As agreed, the price is three dollars thirty-five per kilo." (Conforme combinado)
Finalize -- to complete the last details so something is final
"Let us finalize the shipping schedule this week." (Finalizar / Concluir)
Proceed -- to go ahead with the next part of the deal
"The deal is approved, so we can proceed." (Prosseguir / Seguir em frente)
Next steps -- the actions both sides will take after closing the deal
"Here are the next steps: sign the form and set the date." (Próximos passos)
Deliverable -- a document or result that one side must hand over
"The first deliverable is the signed order form." (Entregável)
Timeline -- the dates and stages in the order they will happen
"The timeline is: sign on Friday, ship next month." (Cronograma / Linha do tempo)
Outstanding -- still open and not yet finished or handed over
"The signed form is the only outstanding item on my side." (Pendente / Em aberto)
Go-ahead -- permission from a boss or client to start something
"My director gave the go-ahead, so we can sign." (Sinal verde / Autorização)
Move forward -- to continue with the deal and the next stages together
"Once the form is signed, we can move forward." (Avançar / Seguir adiante)
Summary -- a short review of the main points that were agreed
"Here is a summary of the deal for your records." (Resumo)
On board -- agreeing with the plan and willing to take part
"Reply to confirm you are on board, and we will sign." (De acordo / A bordo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agreement
As agreed
Finalize
Proceed
Next steps
Deliverable
Timeline
Outstanding
Go-ahead
Move forward
Summary
On board

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the email and answer the questions.

Dear Clara,

Thank you for a fair negotiation. Here is a short summary to confirm our agreement. As agreed, the price is three dollars thirty-five per kilo, with free freight, for twenty containers. You said the deal was approved by your director, and you told me the signed order form was the first deliverable. The form is the only outstanding item on my side, and I will send it by Friday so we can proceed. The next steps and timeline are: sign by Friday, then finalize the shipping schedule and move forward. Please reply to confirm you are on board, and we will both sign off.

Best regards,
Walyson

1. What did Clara's director say about the deal?
A He said the deal was approved.
B He said the deal was cancelled.
C He said nothing about the deal.
2. What is the only outstanding item on Walyson's side?
A The price.
B The signed order form, which he will send by Friday.
C The free freight.
3. What are the next steps after the form is signed?
A Negotiate the price again.
C Withdraw the offer.
B Finalize the shipping schedule and move forward.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported speech

GRAMMAR

Usamos reported speech para contar o que alguém disse, ao confirmar uma negociação por escrito. O verbo recua um tempo (backshift). / We use reported speech to report what someone said, when we confirm a negotiation in writing. The verb moves one step back (backshift).

Direct / DiretoReported / Reportado
"The deal is approved."She said the deal was approved.
"We will finalize it."He said they would finalize it.
"You can proceed."She said we could proceed.
"We have a deal."He said we had a deal.

Key point / Ponto-chave: ao reportar, recue o verbo um tempo (backshift): is/are → was/were, will → would, can → could, has → had. Use say sem pessoa ("she said that...") ou tell + pessoa ("she told me that..."). NUNCA "said me". O that é opcional. / Move the verb one step back; use say (no person) or tell + person; never "said me".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"She me the deal was approved."
"He said the deal approved."
", the price is three thirty-five with free freight."
"The signed form is the only item on my side."
"The first is the signed order form."
"Please reply to confirm you are ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Confirmation Email in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of writing a confirmation email in the correct order.

?After that, report what was decided with reported speech, for example she said the deal was approved.
?First, open the email and thank the other side for the agreement.
?Finally, give a clear deadline, ask the other side to confirm, and say that you will both sign off and move forward.
?Next, confirm the final terms clearly, for example the price, the volume, and the freight, using as agreed.
?Then, list the next steps and the outstanding items, such as the signed order form and the shipping timeline.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

As agreed, the price is three thirty-five with free freight.
Conforme combinado, o preço é três e trinta e cinco com frete grátis.
She said the deal was approved, so we can proceed.
Ela disse que o negócio estava aprovado, então podemos prosseguir.
The signed form is the only outstanding deliverable.
O formulário assinado é o único entregável pendente.
Please reply to confirm you are on board, and we will sign off.
Por favor, responda confirmando que está de acordo, e ambos finalizaremos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to confirm the agreed price in writing. You write:
A "As agreed, the price is three dollars thirty-five per kilo, with free freight."
B "Maybe the price is something around three something."
C "Let us negotiate the price again."
You want to report what Clara's director decided. You write:
A "She said me the deal is approved."
B "You said the deal was approved, so we can proceed."
C "The deal will be maybe approved."
You want to list the next steps and ask the buyer to confirm. You write:
A "Do whatever you want."
B "Next, we will finalize the timeline and move forward. Please reply to confirm you are on board."
C "I withdraw the agreement."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you have just agreed a coffee deal with a buyer at Sucafina. Write a short confirmation email: thank the buyer, confirm the terms with "as agreed", report what was decided with reported speech ("she said the deal was approved"), list the outstanding deliverable and the next steps, and ask the buyer to confirm. Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1As agreed, the price is three thirty-five with free freight.Conforme combinado, o preço é três e trinta e cinco com frete grátis.
2You said the deal was approved, so we can proceed.Você disse que o negócio estava aprovado, então podemos prosseguir.
3The signed form is the only outstanding deliverable.O formulário assinado é o único entregável pendente.
4Next, we will finalize the timeline and move forward.A seguir, vamos finalizar o cronograma e seguir adiante.
5Please reply to confirm you are on board, and we will sign off.Por favor, responda confirmando que está de acordo, e ambos finalizaremos.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Small Talk Before Business -- Building Rapport

Making small talk to build rapport before a meeting starts. Learning open questions (How / What...?) instead of yes/no questions, and follow-ups (What about you?) to keep the conversation going. New vocabulary: small talk, rapport, icebreaker, common ground, weather, weekend, hobby, traffic, jet lag, host, guest, get along.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Small talk -- light, friendly conversation about easy topics, not business
"We made some small talk about the weather before the meeting." (Conversa informal)
Rapport -- a good, friendly connection and trust between people
"A little small talk helps you build rapport with a new client." (Sintonia / Boa relação)
Icebreaker -- a question or comment that starts a friendly conversation
"The flight is a good icebreaker: How was your trip?" (Quebra-gelo)
Common ground -- an interest or experience that two people share
"We both like hiking, so we found common ground quickly." (Interesse em comum)
Weather -- if it is sunny, rainy, hot or cold -- a safe small-talk topic
"The weather is beautiful today, isn't it?" (Tempo / Clima)
Weekend -- Saturday and Sunday, when most people do not work
"How was your weekend? Did you do anything nice?" (Fim de semana)
Hobby -- an activity you do for fun in your free time
"Hiking is my favorite hobby on the weekend." (Passatempo / Hobby)
Traffic -- the cars and buses on the road, often slow in a big city
"The traffic from the airport was heavy this morning." (Trânsito)
Jet lag -- the tired feeling you get after a long flight across time zones
"After the flight from Europe, I had a bit of jet lag." (Fuso horário / Jet lag)
Host -- the person who receives and looks after a visitor
"As the host, I welcomed Clara and offered her a coffee." (Anfitrião)
Guest -- the visitor who is received and looked after by the host
"Clara was our guest, so I made her feel welcome." (Convidado / Visitante)
Get along -- to have a good, friendly relationship with someone
"We get along well, so the meeting was relaxed." (Se dar bem)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Small talk
Rapport
Icebreaker
Common ground
Weather
Weekend
Hobby
Traffic
Jet lag
Host
Guest
Get along

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the short text and answer the questions.

When a client like Clara arrives for a meeting, Walyson does not start with prices. As the host, he makes a little small talk first to build rapport. He breaks the ice with an open question: "How was your flight? Do you have any jet lag?" Then he chooses safe topics -- the weather and the traffic from the airport. To find common ground, he asks an open question about her free time: "What did you do on your weekend?" Clara says she went hiking, her favorite hobby. Walyson loves hiking too, so he follows up: "I love hiking. What about you?" They both relax, and Walyson knows they will get along before they even talk business.

1. Why does Walyson make small talk before the meeting?
A To build rapport with the client first.
B To talk about prices immediately.
C To end the meeting quickly.
2. Which question does Walyson use to break the ice?
A "What is the price per kilo?"
B "How was your flight? Do you have any jet lag?"
C "Did you sign the contract?"
3. What common ground do Walyson and Clara find?
A They both work in finance.
C They both dislike the weather.
B They both love hiking.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Open questions & follow-ups

GRAMMAR

Para small talk, use perguntas ABERTAS (How, What, Why) que pedem uma resposta longa, e não perguntas fechadas (Do, Did, Was) que só pedem sim ou não. Depois de ouvir, use um follow-up para continuar a conversa. / For small talk, use OPEN questions (How, What, Why) that invite a longer answer, not closed yes/no questions. After you listen, use a follow-up to keep the talk going.

Closed / Fechada (yes-no)Open / Aberta (says more)
"Did you have a good trip?""How was your trip?"
"Do you like Santos?""What do you think of Santos?"
"Was your weekend nice?""What did you do on the weekend?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: perguntas abertas começam com How, What, Why, Where, When e pedem uma resposta longa -- ótimas para criar rapport. Depois de ouvir, continue com um follow-up: "What about you?" ou "Really? Tell me more." / Open questions start with How/What/Why and invite a longer answer; keep the talk going with a follow-up like "What about you?"

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" was your flight?"
"I love hiking. What you?"
"A little small talk helps me build ."
"We both enjoy hiking, so we have ."
"The flight was long, so I have a bit of ."
"As the , I welcomed my guest."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of Small Talk in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of good small talk in the correct order.

?After that, ask open questions, not just yes or no questions, so the other person can tell you more.
?First, greet your guest warmly and welcome them as the host.
?Finally, when you have built some rapport and you both get along, move gently from small talk to business.
?Next, break the ice with a safe, easy topic, for example the weather or the trip.
?Then, listen and follow up with a question like what about you, and look for common ground such as a shared hobby.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Welcome to Santos! How was your flight?
Bem-vinda a Santos! Como foi o seu voo?
What did you do on your weekend?
O que você fez no fim de semana?
I love hiking too. What about you?
Eu também adoro fazer trilhas. E você?
So we have common ground. I think we will get along.
Então temos um interesse em comum. Acho que vamos nos dar bem.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

Your client has just arrived. You want to break the ice. You say:
A "Welcome! How was your flight? Do you have any jet lag?"
B "What is your final price per kilo?"
C "Please sign here and we are done."
You want the client to say more about their free time. You ask:
A "Did you have a weekend?"
B "What did you do on your weekend?"
C "Do you have hobbies, yes or no?"
The client says she loves hiking. You also love hiking. You reply:
A "OK. Let us talk about the contract."
B "Really? I love hiking too -- so we have common ground!"
C "I do not have time for hobbies."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a client has just arrived for a meeting at Sucafina, and you are the host. Record a short small talk: welcome your guest, break the ice with an open question (How / What...?), choose a safe topic such as the weather or the trip, ask about their weekend or hobby to find common ground, and use a follow-up like "What about you?". Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1Welcome to Santos! How was your flight?Bem-vinda a Santos! Como foi o seu voo?
2What did you do on your weekend?O que você fez no fim de semana?
3I love hiking too. What about you?Eu também adoro fazer trilhas. E você?
4So we have common ground -- I think we will get along.Então temos um interesse em comum -- acho que vamos nos dar bem.
5It is great to build some rapport. Shall we sit down and start?É ótimo criar uma boa sintonia. Vamos nos sentar e começar?
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Out for Dinner -- Entertaining a Client at a Restaurant

Hosting a client at a restaurant: welcoming your guest, helping with the menu, recommending a dish, and ordering. New grammar: polite requests with "Could you + base verb" and "Would you mind + -ing". New vocabulary: menu, starter, main course, dessert, waiter, order, recommend, dish, bill, tip, spicy, courteous.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Menu -- the list of food and drinks a restaurant offers
"The waiter handed us the menu and we looked at the dishes." (Cardápio)
Starter -- the first, small dish of a meal, before the main course
"For a starter, I will have the tomato soup." (Entrada)
Main course -- the biggest, central dish of a meal
"For my main course, I will have the grilled fish." (Prato principal)
Dessert -- the sweet dish at the end of a meal
"For dessert, would you like some chocolate cake?" (Sobremesa)
Waiter -- the person who serves food and drinks at a restaurant
"The waiter was very polite and brought us some water." (Garçom)
Order -- to tell the waiter what food and drinks you want
"Are you ready to order, or do you need more time?" (Pedir / Fazer o pedido)
Recommend -- to tell someone something is good and they should try it
"I recommend the grilled fish; it is the best dish here." (Recomendar)
Dish -- one specific item of prepared food on the menu
"This dish is a local speciality from Santos." (Prato)
Bill -- the paper that shows how much you have to pay
"As the host, I quietly asked the waiter for the bill." (Conta)
Tip -- extra money you leave for good service
"The service was great, so we left a generous tip." (Gorjeta)
Spicy -- hot, with a strong, peppery taste
"My guest does not like spicy food, so I avoided the chili." (Apimentado)
Courteous -- polite and respectful in the way you treat people
"A courteous host always thanks the waiter." (Cortês / Educado)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Menu
Starter
Main course
Dessert
Waiter
Order
Recommend
Dish
Bill
Tip
Spicy
Courteous

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the short text and answer the questions.

Walyson takes his client Clara to dinner at a restaurant in Santos. As a courteous host, he welcomes her, gives her the menu, and helps her choose. Clara does not eat red meat, so Walyson says: "I would recommend the grilled fish; it is a lovely local dish and it is not spicy." Clara orders the tomato soup as a starter and the fish as a main course, but she skips dessert. Walyson speaks to the waiter politely: instead of "Bring us water," he says "Could you bring us some water?" and "Would you mind taking our order?" At the end of the meal, Walyson quietly asks for the bill and leaves a generous tip, because he is the host.

1. Why does Walyson recommend the grilled fish to Clara?
A Because she does not eat red meat and it is not spicy.
B Because it is the cheapest dish.
C Because she asked for red meat.
2. How does Walyson speak to the waiter?
A "Bring us water now."
B "Could you bring us some water?" and "Would you mind taking our order?"
C He does not speak to the waiter.
3. What does Walyson do at the end of the meal?
A He lets Clara pay the bill.
C He orders another dessert.
B He quietly asks for the bill and leaves a tip.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite requests (Could you / Would you mind)

GRAMMAR

Para fazer pedidos educados (a um garçom, a um cliente), use COULD YOU + verbo na forma base (Could you bring...?) ou WOULD YOU MIND + verbo no -ing (Would you mind bringing...?). Pedidos diretos como "Bring water" soam como uma ordem. / To make polite requests, use COULD YOU + base verb, or WOULD YOU MIND + the -ing form. Direct requests like "Bring water" sound like an order.

Direct / Direto (an order)Polite / Educado
"Bring us water.""Could you bring us some water?"
"Give me the menu.""Would you mind giving me the menu?"
"Take the fish.""I would recommend the fish."

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de Could you use o verbo na forma base (Could you bring...?); depois de Would you mind use a forma -ing (Would you mind bringing...?). Para recomendar um prato, diga "I would recommend the...". / After Could you, use the base verb; after Would you mind, use the -ing form. To suggest a dish, say "I would recommend the..."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you us some water, please?"
"Would you mind us the bill?"
"I would the grilled fish."
"For a , I will have the tomato soup."
"My guest does not like food."
"A good host is always polite and ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of Hosting a Dinner in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of hosting a client at dinner in the correct order.

?After that, use polite requests with the waiter, for example could you bring us some water, instead of just bring water.
?First, welcome your guest at the restaurant and let them choose a comfortable seat.
?Finally, quietly ask for the bill and take care of it, because you are the host who invited the client.
?Next, hand them the menu and offer a recommendation, especially if they trust your taste.
?Then, during the meal, make light conversation and check that your guest is enjoying the food.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Welcome! Please take a seat. Here is the menu.
Bem-vinda! Por favor, sente-se. Aqui está o cardápio.
I would recommend the grilled fish; it is not spicy.
Eu recomendaria o peixe grelhado; não é apimentado.
Could you bring us some water, please?
Você poderia nos trazer um pouco de água, por favor?
Would you mind bringing us the bill, please?
Você se importaria de nos trazer a conta, por favor?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want the waiter to bring water. As a courteous host, you say:
A "Could you bring us some water, please?"
B "Bring water now."
C "Water. Fast."
Your client does not eat red meat. You recommend a main course:
A "Take the beef steak."
B "I would recommend the grilled fish; it is not spicy."
C "You must eat red meat."
The dinner is over and you are the host. You say to the waiter:
A "Give me the bill."
B "Would you mind bringing us the bill, please?"
C "Let my guest pay the bill."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you take a client from Sucafina to dinner, and you are the host. Record a short dinner scene: welcome your guest, hand them the menu, recommend a dish, place the order with polite requests (Could you...? / Would you mind + -ing), and ask for the bill at the end. Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1Welcome! Please take a seat. Here is the menu.Bem-vinda! Por favor, sente-se. Aqui está o cardápio.
2I would recommend the grilled fish; it is not spicy.Eu recomendaria o peixe grelhado; não é apimentado.
3Could you bring us some water, please?Você poderia nos trazer um pouco de água, por favor?
4Would you mind bringing us the bill, please?Você se importaria de nos trazer a conta, por favor?
5It is my pleasure to host you. I will take care of it.É um prazer recebê-la. Eu cuido disso.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Saying Goodbye -- Ending the Visit & Keeping in Touch

Ending a client's visit with a warm goodbye and a plan to keep in touch: thanking your guest, talking about the future, and seeing them off. New grammar: "be going to" for future plans and intentions. New vocabulary: farewell, see off, grateful, memorable, hospitality, productive, fruitful, stay in touch, stop by, looking ahead, sincerely, regards.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Farewell -- a goodbye, especially when someone is leaving for a long time
"We gave Clara a warm farewell at the end of her visit." (Despedida)
See off -- to go with someone to the place they are leaving from, to say goodbye
"I went to the airport to see Clara off before her flight." (Acompanhar / Despedir-se de alguém que parte)
Grateful -- feeling thankful, wanting to say thank you for something
"I am very grateful for the chance to work with you this week." (Grato / Agradecido)
Memorable -- so good or special that you will remember it for a long time
"The dinner last night was truly memorable." (Memorável / Inesquecível)
Hospitality -- the friendly, generous way you treat a guest or visitor
"Thank you for your wonderful hospitality during my visit." (Hospitalidade)
Productive -- getting a lot of useful work or good results done
"It was a very productive week; we finished the whole plan." (Produtivo)
Fruitful -- producing good, useful results; successful
"Our talks were fruitful, and we agreed on the main terms." (Frutífero / Proveitoso)
Stay in touch -- to keep contacting each other after you part
"Let us stay in touch by email after you go home." (Manter contato)
Stop by -- to make a short, casual visit to a place or person
"Please stop by our office in Geneva the next time you travel." (Dar uma passada / Visitar rapidamente)
Looking ahead -- thinking about and planning for the future
"Looking ahead, I am sure our companies are going to grow together." (Pensando no futuro / Olhando adiante)
Sincerely -- a formal, polite way to end a letter or email, before your name
"I closed the thank-you email with Sincerely, Walyson Silva." (Atenciosamente)
Regards -- friendly good wishes, often used to close a message (Best regards)
"Please give my best regards to your team in Geneva." (Cumprimentos / Saudações)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Farewell
Grateful
Memorable
Hospitality
Productive
Stay in touch
Sincerely

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the short text and answer the questions.

Clara's visit to Santos is over, and Walyson goes to the airport to see her off. It has been a memorable and productive week, and Walyson is very grateful for the chance to host her. Clara thanks him for his warm hospitality and says it was a truly fruitful trip. Now they talk about the future. Walyson says: "Next week I am going to send you the final figures, and I am going to call my manager about the next steps." Clara replies: "We are going to stay in touch by email, and next year you must stop by our office in Geneva." Looking ahead, they are both sure their companies are going to build a long relationship. Walyson is going to write a thank-you email and close it with Sincerely and best regards. Then he gives Clara a warm farewell.

1. What is Walyson going to do next week?
A He is going to send the figures and call his manager.
B He is going to cancel the deal.
C He is going to visit Geneva tomorrow.
2. How are Walyson and Clara going to keep in contact?
A They are not going to keep in contact.
B They are going to stay in touch by email.
C Only by phone, every day.
3. How does Walyson close his thank-you email?
A With no closing at all.
C With "Bye now."
B With "Sincerely" and best regards.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- be going to (future plans)

GRAMMAR

Para falar de planos e intenções que você já decidiu, use SUJEITO + am/is/are + GOING TO + verbo na forma base (I am going to send the report). É o tempo dos planos: o que você vai fazer a seguir. / To talk about plans and intentions you already decided, use SUBJECT + am/is/are + GOING TO + base verb. It is the tense of plans: what you are going to do next.

Form / FormaExample / Exemplo
Affirmative / Afirmativa"I am going to send the report."
Negative / Negativa"She is not going to miss the flight."
Question / Pergunta"Are you going to visit us soon?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use am com I, is com he/she/it, are com you/we/they. Depois de going to, sempre o verbo na forma base (send, stay, visit), nunca "to send". / Use am/is/are correctly, and always the base verb after going to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I send you the figures next week."
"Let us by email."
"Thank you for your wonderful ."
"I am very for the chance to work with you."
"It was a truly trip."
"Please our office in Geneva."

Stage 2: Put the Steps of a Good Goodbye in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of saying a good goodbye to a client in the correct order.

?After that, talk about the future using be going to, for example I am going to send you the report and we are going to stay in touch.
?First, thank your guest sincerely for the visit and say how productive it has been.
?Finally, see your guest off, wish them safe travels, and say a warm farewell.
?Next, mention one memorable moment from the week to make the goodbye warm and personal.
?Then, exchange contact details and agree how you are going to keep in touch.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Thank you for a memorable and productive visit.
Obrigado por uma visita memorável e produtiva.
I am going to send you the report next week.
Vou te enviar o relatório na semana que vem.
Let us stay in touch by email.
Vamos manter contato por e-mail.
Have a safe flight, and a warm farewell.
Tenha um bom voo, e uma calorosa despedida.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

Your client is leaving after a great week. You thank her:
A "Thank you for a memorable and productive visit. I am very grateful."
B "Goodbye. That is all."
C "You can leave now."
You want to tell her your plan for next week:
A "I going to send the report."
B "I am going to send you the final figures next week."
C "I am going to to send the report."
You want to keep the relationship alive after she goes home:
A "We will never speak again."
B "Let us stay in touch by email, and please stop by our office again."
C "Do not contact me."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a client from Sucafina is leaving after a week in Santos, and you are seeing them off. Record a short farewell: thank them for the visit, mention one memorable moment, share two future plans using be going to ("I am going to..."), and agree how you are going to stay in touch. Use at least four of today's words or phrases.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Thank you for a memorable and productive visit.Obrigado por uma visita memorável e produtiva.
2I am going to send you the report next week.Vou te enviar o relatório na semana que vem.
3Let us stay in touch by email.Vamos manter contato por e-mail.
4Please stop by our office whenever you travel here.Dê uma passada no nosso escritório sempre que vier aqui.
5Have a safe flight, and a warm farewell.Tenha um bom voo, e uma calorosa despedida.

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Who Is Walyson? — Diagnostic + Self-Introduction
First Impressions at the Coffee Summit -- 40 slides
02
My Workday at Sucafina
Daily Routines and Adverbs of Frequency -- 28 slides
03
The Coffee Supply Chain
Passive Voice and the Journey from Origin to Cup -- 28 slides
04
Meeting the Team
Introductions and the Present Continuous for Projects -- 28 slides
05
Emails That Get Results
Professional Emails, Formal Register and Hedging Language -- 28 slides
06
Numbers, Prices, and Contracts
Large Numbers, Percentages and Comparatives -- 28 slides
07
Reporting to the Director
Status Updates, Present Perfect and Linking Words -- 28 slides
08
Handling Phone Calls
Phone Expressions and Indirect Questions -- 28 slides
09
Looking Back, Moving Forward
Review and Self-Assessment: used to / would -- 28 slides
10
Meetings That Matter
Modal Verbs: should, could, might -- 28 slides
11
Agreeing and Disagreeing
Diplomatic disagreement and softeners -- 28 slides
12
Making Presentations
Signposting language and sequencing to deliver a 5-minute talk -- 28 slides
13
Negotiating a Deal
Conditional language (if / unless / provided) to bargain on price and terms -- 28 slides
14
Reading Contracts
The language of clauses and obligations -- shall / must / be required to -- 28 slides
15
Writing a Formal Email
Requesting and confirming contract terms in writing -- polite request and confirmation language -- 28 slides
16
Negotiating by Email
Making and responding to written offers and counteroffers -- the second conditional -- 28 slides
17
Closing the Deal
Confirming the final agreement and next steps in writing -- reported speech -- 28 slides
18
Small Talk Before Business
Building rapport before the meeting starts -- open questions and follow-ups -- 28 slides
19
Out for Dinner
Entertaining a client at a restaurant -- polite requests and recommendations -- 28 slides
20
Saying Goodbye
Ending the visit and keeping in touch -- be going to for future plans -- 28 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Aula 1 — First Impressions

Série
Succession — Season 1, Episode 1: "Celebration"

Vocabulário corporativo de alto escalão, dinâmicas de poder e apresentações profissionais. Observe como os personagens se apresentam e usam títulos profissionais. Conexão com a aula: linguagem de hierarquia ("I report to", "he oversees") em ambiente multinacional.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 3 expressões que os personagens usam para se apresentar.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
How I Built This — Episode: "Starbucks: Howard Schultz"

Howard Schultz conta como transformou a Starbucks de uma pequena loja em Seattle numa multinacional global de café. Vocabulário de commodities, sourcing e negociação. Conexão direta com o universo do Walyson na Sucafina.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 20 minutos. Anote como Schultz descreve o processo de sourcing de café.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
TED Talk — "How to Speak So That People Want to Listen" (Julian Treasure)

Habilidades de comunicação que fazem as pessoas prestarem atenção. Relevante para apresentações profissionais e networking. Conexão com a aula: como causar uma boa primeira impressão ao se apresentar.

Dica: assista com legendas em inglês ativadas. Pratique repetir 2-3 frases que o palestrante usa.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 -- My Workday at Sucafina

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A whole day at a paper company, from the morning commute to the end-of-shift wrap-up. Listen for how people describe what they usually, often, and always do at work.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five sentences that use a frequency adverb (always, usually, often, sometimes, never). Then describe your own Monday at Sucafina using three of them.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Talking About Your Daily Routine"

A short, clear episode on describing a typical day in the present simple, with natural use of adverbs of frequency. Perfect for picking up the rhythm and word stress you are training this week.

Record a 90-second audio describing your workday: your commute, your shift, your deadlines, and how you wrap up. Use at least four new words from today.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Adverbs of Frequency"

A focused lesson on where adverbs of frequency go in a sentence: before the main verb, but after the verb "to be". Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six true sentences about your routine at Sucafina, one for each adverb: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never. Read them aloud with the correct word stress.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 -- The Coffee Supply Chain

Documentary
Netflix -- "Rotten" (Season 2): The Bitter Truth (coffee episode)

A documentary that follows coffee along its supply chain, from the growers and the harvest to the export market. Listen for how each step of the process is described.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five passive sentences you hear (for example, "the coffee is grown..."). Then describe one step of the chain in your own words using the passive voice.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Cup of Excellence -- "From Farm to Cup"

A short, clear episode on how green coffee is harvested, milled, graded, and exported. Perfect for hearing the supply-chain vocabulary and the passive voice used naturally by industry people.

Record a 90-second audio explaining the journey of your coffee from origin to cup. Use at least four new words from today and the passive voice at every step.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The Passive Voice"

A focused lesson on how the passive is formed (to be + past participle) and when to use it to describe a process. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six true sentences about how coffee is produced, one passive form for each step: grown, harvested, milled, graded, exported, roasted. Read them aloud with the correct stress.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 -- Meeting the Team

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A workplace comedy where colleagues introduce themselves, describe their roles, and talk about what they are doing at work. Listen for introductions and the present continuous.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five present continuous sentences you hear (for example, "he is working on..."). Then introduce one character: their role and what they are doing.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Introducing Your Team"

A short, clear episode on how to introduce colleagues, describe their roles, and explain what each person is in charge of. Perfect for hearing the team vocabulary and the present continuous used naturally.

Record a 90-second audio introducing your team. Name three people, give each role and department, and say what each one is working on, using the present continuous.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The Present Continuous"

A focused lesson on how the present continuous is formed (to be + verb-ing) and when to use it for actions happening now. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six true sentences about your team right now, one for each person: who is leading, handling, preparing, setting up, reaching out, and launching. Read them aloud with the correct stress.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 -- Emails That Get Results

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A law firm where professionals write and discuss formal messages, make polite requests, and follow up on important matters. Listen for the polite, formal register used at work.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three polite requests you hear (for example, "could you...", "would it be possible to..."). Then rewrite one direct sentence to make it more polite.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Writing Professional Emails"

A clear episode on how to open, structure, and close a professional email, with polite phrases for making requests, confirming details, and following up.
