Record a 90-second audio dictating a request email. Include a subject line, a polite opening, a soft request, the attachment, and a polite closing, using "could you" and "I was wondering if".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Polite Requests and Hedging"

A focused lesson on softening requests with modals (could, would) and polite phrases (I was wondering if, would it be possible to). Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six polite requests for work, one for each verb: send, confirm, clarify, forward, reply, and acknowledge. Read them aloud with a calm, polite tone.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 -- Numbers, Prices, and Contracts

Series
Billions -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A finance world where professionals talk about prices, deals, percentages and numbers under pressure. Listen for how they compare figures and negotiate.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three comparatives you hear (for example, "higher than", "more expensive than") and one sentence with a percentage.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Talking About Numbers and Trends"

A clear episode on saying large numbers and percentages, and on describing prices that increase or decrease using comparatives.

Record a 90-second audio giving a price quote: say the rate in dollars, compare it with the market, offer a discount for a large volume, and predict a percentage increase.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Comparatives and Big Numbers"

A focused lesson on comparative adjectives (cheaper, more expensive, better) and on reading large numbers and percentages aloud. Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, read these figures aloud: 4,200; 3,900; 8%; 10%. Then build six sentences comparing two prices, one for each verb: increase, decrease, and using cheaper, higher, lower, better.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 -- Reporting to the Director

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 2, Episode 1: "The Dundies"

Watch how managers and staff report progress, give quick updates and brief each other in a real workplace. Notice how they talk about what has already happened.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences in the present perfect that you hear (for example, "I have finished", "we have done") and one linking word (however, therefore, in addition).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Giving a Status Update at Work"

A clear episode on reporting progress to a manager: using the present perfect for results and linking words to connect a problem with its solution.

Record a 2-minute status update on a real project: give an overview, say what you have already achieved, highlight one delay or pending task, and use at least two linking words.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The Present Perfect Tense"

A focused lesson on have/has + past participle, and on the words already, yet and so far. Directly reinforces today's grammar for status updates.

After watching, build six sentences about your week using the present perfect: three affirmative with "already" and three negative with "yet". Then link two of them with "however" and "therefore".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 -- Handling Phone Calls

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 2: "Errors and Omissions"

Watch how lawyers and assistants handle business phone calls: answering, putting people through, taking messages and asking for information politely.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three phone expressions you hear (for example, hold on, put you through, leave a message) and one polite question.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Telephone English: Making and Taking Calls"

A clear episode on phone language: answering professionally, asking the caller to hold on, transferring calls, and using indirect questions to sound polite.

Record a 2-minute phone role-play: answer a call, say one company is not available, offer to take a message, and ask for a detail using an indirect question.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Indirect Questions: Being Polite"

A focused lesson on indirect questions, such as Could you tell me...? and Do you know if...?. It directly reinforces today's grammar for polite phone calls.

After watching, turn six direct questions into indirect ones. Three should start with Could you tell me and three with Do you know if.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 -- Looking Back, Moving Forward

Series
The Last Dance -- Episode 1 (documentary)

Watch how an elite team reflects on its journey, talks about strengths and weaknesses, and sets goals for the future. Notice how people compare how things used to be with how they are now.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences with used to or would, and one strength and one weakness you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
All Ears English -- "How to Talk About Your Progress and Goals"

A clear episode on reflecting in English: describing your strengths and weaknesses, giving and receiving feedback, and setting goals to improve.

Record a 2-minute self-assessment: how you used to speak, how you speak now, your strength, your weakness, and one goal for the next block.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "used to, would and the past"

A focused lesson on used to and would for past habits and states. It directly reinforces today's grammar for comparing the past with now.

After watching, write six sentences about your English journey: three with used to and three with would.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 -- Meetings That Matter

Series
The Office (US) -- a team meeting scene

Watch a workplace meeting and notice how the chair opens the meeting, follows an agenda, and makes suggestions. Listen for should, could and might when people propose ideas.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three suggestions you hear with should, could or might, and one meeting word, such as agenda or recap.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
All Ears English -- "How to Make Suggestions in Meetings"

A clear episode on running and joining meetings in English: opening with an agenda, making polite suggestions, and reaching consensus before you adjourn.

Record a 2-minute meeting: open with the agenda, make three suggestions with should, could and might, allocate one action item, and recap before you adjourn.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "should, could and might (modals)"

A focused lesson on the modal verbs should, could and might for advice, options and possibility. It directly reinforces today's grammar for making suggestions in a meeting.

After watching, write six meeting sentences: two with should, two with could, and two with might.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 -- Agreeing and Disagreeing

Series
Suits -- a negotiation or debate scene

Watch two characters argue a business decision. Notice how they share a point of view, push back politely, and look for a compromise instead of just saying "no".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two softeners you hear (for example "I see your point, but...") and one phrase used to disagree.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
All Ears English -- "How to Disagree Politely in English"

A practical episode on diplomatic disagreement: softeners, conceding a valid point, and reaching a compromise so the conversation stays respectful.

Record a 2-minute debate: share your point of view, agree with one valid part, push back with a softener, and suggest a compromise.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Agreeing and disagreeing politely"

A focused lesson on the phrases for agreeing, softening, and disagreeing diplomatically in meetings and debates. It directly reinforces today's language.

After watching, write six sentences: two to agree, two to soften and push back, and two to suggest a compromise.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 -- Making Presentations

TED Talk
TED -- a short business or data talk (under 6 minutes)

Watch a speaker open with a clear outline and move through their points. Notice how they signpost with "first", "then" and "finally", and how they sum up the key takeaway at the end.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three signposts the speaker uses and the one key takeaway you remember.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
All Ears English -- "Signposting Language for Clear Presentations"

A practical episode on structuring a talk: giving the outline, moving on between points, walking the audience through a visual aid, and summing up the key takeaway.

Record a 5-minute briefing: open with the outline, signpost three points with first, then and finally, and sum up your message.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Signposting language in presentations"

A focused lesson on the phrases that guide an audience through a talk: opening with an outline, sequencing points, and closing before the Q and A. It directly reinforces today's language.

After watching, write six sentences: one outline, three signposted points (first, then, finally), one to walk through a visual aid, and one to sum up.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 -- Negotiating a Deal

Series
Suits -- a negotiation scene (any season)

Watch a scene where two sides bargain over terms. Notice how each side states a bottom line, uses leverage, and offers a trade-off before they meet halfway.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one conditional offer ("If you..., I will...") and one deal-breaker you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Negotiate Anything -- "Conditional Offers and Trade-offs"

A practical episode on how to bargain: knowing your bottom line, using leverage, making offers with conditions, and closing in good faith without reaching a deadlock.

Record three conditional offers for a coffee contract: one with "if", one with "unless", and one with "provided".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Conditionals: if, unless and provided"

A focused lesson on the first conditional and the words unless and provided. It explains the structure (present simple in the condition, will in the result) and directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six sentences: two with "if", two with "unless", and two with "provided", all about a price negotiation.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 -- Reading Contracts

Series
Suits -- a contract-signing scene (any season)

Watch a scene where lawyers read a contract clause by clause before signing. Notice how they talk about obligations, a possible breach, and who is liable.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one obligation ("X shall...") and one word for a broken rule (breach) that you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "How to Read a Business Contract"

A practical episode on the parts of a contract: clauses, obligations, warranties and liability, and the dates that make a contract binding.

Record three sentences about a coffee contract: one with "shall", one with "must", and one with "be required to".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Modals of obligation: must, have to, shall"

A focused lesson on the modals that express obligation and how "shall" is used in formal and legal English. It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six contract sentences: two with "shall", two with "must", and two with "be required to".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 -- Writing a Formal Email

Series
Suits -- an email or letter-writing scene (any season)

Watch a scene where a character sends a formal business email or letter to confirm terms. Notice how they open, make polite requests, and sign off.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one opening line ("I am writing to...") and one polite request you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "How to Write a Professional Email"

A practical episode on the structure of a formal business email: the opening line, polite requests, enclosing documents, and a professional sign-off.

Record three formal email lines: one opening ("I am writing to..."), one request ("Would you kindly..."), and one close ("I look forward to...").

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Polite requests in formal English"

A focused lesson on polite request language: would you, could you, I would be grateful if you could, and kindly. It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six formal email sentences: two requests, two confirmations, and two sign-offs.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 -- Negotiating by Email

Series
Suits -- a price or deal negotiation scene (any season)

Watch a scene where a character negotiates terms and counters an offer. Notice how they justify a position, make a counterproposal, and reach a final figure.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one counterproposal and one "final offer" line you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "How to Negotiate Politely in English"

A practical episode on bargaining language: making offers and counteroffers, using conditional sentences, and closing on a fair figure by email.

Record three counterproposal lines with the second conditional: "If you raised..., I would...", "If you covered..., I would...".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The second conditional"

A focused lesson on the second conditional: if + past simple, would + base verb, for imaginary situations. It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six second-conditional sentences to negotiate a price: three offers and three counteroffers.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 -- Closing the Deal

Series
Suits -- a deal-closing scene (any season)

Watch a scene where characters close a deal and confirm the terms. Notice how they summarize the agreement and report what was decided.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one line that confirms an agreement and one reported-speech line ("he said... was...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "How to Confirm a Deal Clearly in English"

A practical episode on closing language: confirming terms, summarizing the agreement, and reporting what was decided so nothing is lost.

Record three confirmation lines: one with "as agreed", one with reported speech, and one with "next steps".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Reported speech"

A focused lesson on reported speech: say and tell, and the backshift of verbs (is to was, will to would, can to could). It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six reported-speech sentences about a deal: three with "said" and three with "told me".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 -- Small Talk Before Business

Series
Ted Lasso -- a "getting to know you" scene (any season)

Watch a scene where characters break the ice and build rapport with small talk before getting to the point. Notice the open questions and the friendly follow-ups.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two open questions (How / What...?) and one follow-up like "What about you?".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "How to Make Small Talk in English"

A practical episode on small talk: how to break the ice, choose safe topics, ask open questions, and find common ground to build rapport before business.

Record three small-talk lines: one icebreaker, one open question about the weekend, and one follow-up.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Open and closed questions"

A focused lesson on open vs. closed questions: how How/What/Why questions invite longer answers, while yes/no questions stop the conversation. It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, turn six closed questions into open questions, and write two follow-ups.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 -- Out for Dinner

Series
The Bear -- a restaurant dinner-service scene (any season)

Watch a scene set in a restaurant. Notice how staff and guests order, recommend dishes, and ask for things politely with "Could you...?" and "Would you mind...?".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two polite requests and one recommendation ("I would recommend...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "Ordering Food and Being Polite in English"

A practical episode on restaurant English: how to read a menu, order a starter and a main course, recommend dishes, and ask for the bill politely.

Record three lines: one recommendation, one "Could you...?" request, and one "Would you mind + -ing" request.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Polite requests: Could you and Would you mind"

A focused lesson on polite requests: how "Could you + base verb" and "Would you mind + -ing" make you sound courteous. It directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, turn six direct orders into polite requests, using both forms.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 -- Saying Goodbye

Series
Suits -- a client farewell or send-off scene (any season)

Watch a scene where a business relationship is closed warmly. Notice how people thank each other, talk about future plans, and agree to keep in touch.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two sentences with "going to" and one phrase used to say goodbye.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Plain English -- "Saying Goodbye and Staying in Touch in English"

A practical episode on farewells: how to thank a host, talk about future plans, and keep a professional relationship alive after a visit.

Record three lines: one "I am going to..." plan, one thank-you, and one way to stay in touch.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Talking about the future: be going to"

A focused lesson on "be going to" for plans and intentions. It directly reinforces today's grammar for talking about what you are going to do next.

After watching, write six sentences about your real plans for next week, all with "be going to".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Welcome Key Words The Code Hotel Arts Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
LESSON 1
T
